EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of July 14th, a cold front passed through Northwestern Kansas allowing for thunderstorms to move through the region. One of these thunderstorms became severe and produced wind gusts up to 70 mph along with quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought status improved slightly for some of the counties in Northwest Kansas toward the end of the month.  Rainfall amounts ranged from two to eight inches, with the highest amounts falling over Gove County.  Normal rainfall for the month is three to four inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of July 15th, showers and thunderstorms that developed in the Front Range of the Rocky Mountains moved eastward into Northwestern Kansas. One of these thunderstorms became severe and produced hail up to quarters in size as well as wind gusts up to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported 60 mph thunderstorm wind gusts along with quarter sized hail at location for a four minute duration. Spotter's anemometer was damaged by hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon a narrow corridor of dust was blowing across Highway 27 in southern Cheyenne County.  The dust was thick enough to lower the visibility to less than a quarter mile for a few hundred yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly eight miles south of Wheeler a corridor of dust was being blown across Highway 27.  The dust was coming from a bare field next to the highway.  The corridor of dust was a few hundred yards wide, with visibility under a quarter mile in the dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions improved across portions southeastern Kansas through the end of May. Beneficial rainfall allowed drought conditions to improve over most of southeastern Kansas during the month. Across southeastern Kansas Extreme (D3) drought was removed  thanks to several rounds of rainfall. Severe (D2) Drought persisted however with Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) around the Severe Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month proved beneficial for much of the county improving from Extreme (D3) Drought, though drought conditions remained across the region through the month of May. The US Drought Monitor indicated the southern half of the county as remaining in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Early spring crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A linear cluster of storms developed off the Front Range moving east across Northwest Kansas during the evening hours. The storms produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 100 mph as they moved through, with the strongest gusts reported in the town of McDonald in Rawlins County. Damage occurred primarily to trees in portions of Cheyenne and Rawlins counties with additional damage to powerlines, campers, and structures. The thunderstorm wind gusts and the outflow of the storm also created dust storm conditions with visibilities of one-quarter mile or less observed in Cheyenne, Sherman, and Rawlins counties. Blowing dust with visibility reductions of three to five miles were also observed near the cities of Colby and Oakley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated 70-80 mph caused many tree limbs to be blown down of various sizes, shingles torn off a building, and 2x4s blown out of a truck bed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a prolonged period of freezing drizzle to the area, beginning during the early overnight hours of January 29th and continuing throughout the day on January 29th before ending in the early overnight hours of January 30th. Cherokee County also saw some light sleet briefly move through from mid morning to mid day on January 30th. While no observed reports of ice accretions or sleet were received with this event, the Kansas Department of Transportation traveler map showed partly covered roadways due to the ice and sleet, causing slick spots on roadways. Several schools were closed on January 30th due to the winter road impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour period of freezing drizzle began shortly after midnight on January 29th and continued until the early overnight hours of January 30th. While no observed reports of ice accretions or sleet were received with this event, the Kansas Department of Transportation traveler map showed partly covered roadways due to the freezing drizzle, causing slick spots on roadways. Several schools were closed on January 30th due to the winter road impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a prolonged period of freezing drizzle to the area, beginning during the early overnight hours of January 29th and continuing throughout the day on January 29th before ending in the early overnight hours of January 30th. Cherokee County also saw some light sleet briefly move through from mid morning to mid day on January 30th. While no observed reports of ice accretions or sleet were received with this event, the Kansas Department of Transportation traveler map showed partly covered roadways due to the ice and sleet, causing slick spots on roadways. Several schools were closed on January 30th due to the winter road impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle began shortly after midnight on January 29th and continued until the early overnight hours of January 30th. There was then a brief break in precipitation before some brief, light sleet moved into the county from mid morning until mid day on January 30th. While no observed reports of ice accretions or sleet were received with this event, the Kansas Department of Transportation traveler map showed partly covered roadways due to the freezing drizzle and sleet, causing slick spots on roadways. Several schools were closed on January 30th due to the winter road impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system lifted northeast from Oklahoma through Arkansas and Missouri on January 24th and 25th, bringing widespread precipitation to southeast Kansas beginning as rain during the afternoon of January 24th before quickly changing over to snow. This snow continued into the morning hours of January 25th before coming to an end as the storm system exited the region. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from around 1 to 2 inches across southeast Kansas during this event, leading to partly covered roadways as well as some school closings on January 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed over to all snow during the late afternoon hours of January 24th. Estimated snowfall amounts across the county ranged from 1 to 2 inches. KDOT reported roads became partly covered during the event. Several schools closed on January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought heavy snow to Northwest Kansas.  Snowfall amounts ranged from a couple inches in northwest Cheyenne County to nine inches in Sherman, Thomas, Wallace, Logan, Greeley, and Wichita counties.  Unlike most snow events, this one had little to no blowing snow with it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were eight to nine inches across the county.||Key Impacts:  snow packed roadways, limited visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system moved east across East-Central Colorado into Northwest Kansas producing freezing fog, freezing rain, and light to heavy snow over the region. The lowest visibility reports ranged from near-zero to one-quarter mile across Northwest Kansas with zero accidents reported. Moisture from the freezing fog and freezing rain on the 2nd lead to ice accumulations up to one-quarter inch mainly in Rawlins and Logan counties. The heaviest snowfall accumulations were mainly along and north of Highway 36 with isolated areas of three to five inches between Highways 25 and 83. Key impacts: reduced visibility, heavy snow, and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a period of 18 hours, Logan County experienced freezing fog, freezing rain, and light to moderate snow. During the day on the 2nd, the fog produced visibilities as low as one-quarter mile with moisture from the fog and following freezing rain creating ice accumulations up to one-quarter inch. The only reports of ice accumulation came from city of Oakley, Kansas. During the afternoon to evening hours, light to moderate snow produced one to five inches of snow over the county. The highest snow amounts were reported in the northeast corner of the county in the city of Oakley. Snow drifts up to a foot were also observed. Key impacts: reduced visibility, heavy snow, and hazardous travel conditions.

The greatest ice accumulations occurred along Highway 36 between Atwood and Herndon. During the evening hours through the early morning hours on the 3rd, heavy snow produced three to eight inches of snow over the county. The highest snow amounts were reported in the northeast side of the county near the town of Ludell, Kansas. Key impacts: reduced visibility, heavy snow, and hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system lifted northeast from Oklahoma through Arkansas and Missouri on January 24th and 25th, bringing widespread precipitation to southeast Kansas beginning as rain during the afternoon of January 24th before quickly changing over to snow. This snow continued into the morning hours of January 25th before coming to an end as the storm system exited the region. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from around 1 to 2 inches across southeast Kansas during this event, leading to partly covered roadways as well as some school closings on January 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed over to all snow during the late afternoon hours of January 24th. A snow report of 1.8 inches was reported at a CoCoRaHS station 0.8 miles NNE of Farlington. KDOT reported roads became partly covered during the event. Several schools closed on January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought heavy snow to Northwest Kansas.  Snowfall amounts ranged from a couple inches in northwest Cheyenne County to nine inches in Sherman, Thomas, Wallace, Logan, Greeley, and Wichita counties.  Unlike most snow events, this one had little to no blowing snow with it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from six to eight inches across the county from north to south.  The highest snow amounts were reported in the southwest part of the county.  ||Key Impacts:  snow packed roadways, limited visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southwest upper-level low made its way south of the CWA putting a good bullseye across southwest Kansas for snowfall. Ahead of the frontal and around the occlusion, many reports of 2-10 inches. Amplified by frontogenic lift within the surface boundary and ample low-level moisture, snow continued through the afternoon hours before ending and moving northeast towards sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 7 inches of snow at Cimarron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening hours of January 17th, a low-pressure system from southeast Colorado traveled northeast across northwest Kansas and on into south central Nebraska. As this system trekked through the region, an initial snowfall occurred on the leading edge of the system dropping up to 5 inches of snow overnight on the 17th. During the day on the 18th, precipitation began to wrap around the system and create additional snowfall. In Norton county Kansas, 8.5 inches of snow was the highest amount of snowfall recorded from this system in northwest Kansas. There was about a 4 hour break for the heavier snowfall in the middle to later half of the event before the snow increased intensity again. The break lasted about 4 hours near Norton county, with the break being shorter farther west.  I-70 was closed for both directions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the morning of the 19th, the public reported 8 inches of storm total snowfall in the south central portion of the county. This was the highest amount of snowfall reported in Cheyenne County with other locations near the center of the county reported around 4 inches of snow. The AWOS in St. Francis reported multiple gusts over 20 mph which created blowing snow reducing visibility down to 0.25 mile at times. Key impacts: heavy snow, blowing snow, snow packed roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed towards the CWA late in the afternoon tightening the gradient ahead of it with strong downsloping winds from the southwest gusting up to 50-65 mph for southwestern counties in lee of the deepening surface low over eastern Colorado. Points to the north and east gusted up to 40-50 mph but below high wind warning criteria. Behind the cold front northwest winds gusted up to 40-50 mph in these areas for another hour or two into the evening hours after sunset before subsiding to 25-35 mph by midnight and less than 15-20 mph by sunrise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought heavy snow to Northwest Kansas.  Snowfall amounts ranged from a couple inches in northwest Cheyenne County to nine inches in Sherman, Thomas, Wallace, Logan, Greeley, and Wichita counties.  Unlike most snow events, this one had little to no blowing snow with it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were around eight inches across the county.  The eight inches were reported at both Atwood and near Beardsley.||Key Impacts:  snow packed roadways, limited visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening hours of January 17th, a low-pressure system from southeast Colorado traveled northeast across northwest Kansas and on into south central Nebraska. As this system trekked through the region, an initial snowfall occurred on the leading edge of the system dropping up to 5 inches of snow overnight on the 17th. During the day on the 18th, precipitation began to wrap around the system and create additional snowfall. In Norton county Kansas, 8.5 inches of snow was the highest amount of snowfall recorded from this system in northwest Kansas. There was about a 4 hour break for the heavier snowfall in the middle to later half of the event before the snow increased intensity again. The break lasted about 4 hours near Norton county, with the break being shorter farther west.  I-70 was closed for both directions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the evening of the 18th, the public reported 8.5 inches of snow in the eastern portion of the county. There are few reports from Norton county from this event, but it seems like most of the county received over 6 inches of snow. Winds at the McCook, Nebraska ASOS occasionally gust to 30 mph and frequently over 25 mph during the storm. These winds likely caused blowing snow that reduced visibility down to 0.25 mile multiple times during the event in Norton county. Key impacts: heavy snow, blowing snow, snow packed roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the night of January 30th and lasting into the morning of January 31st, portions of Northwestern Kansas saw cold temperatures as low as -12 degrees Fahrenheit and westerly winds up to around 12 mph. These conditions allowed for extremely cold wind chill values as low as -28 degrees Fahrenheit within the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values at -25 degrees Fahrenheit or below were recorded within Thomas county. The lowest wind chill value of -28 degrees Fahrenheit was recorded at an AWOS located 3 miles north of Colby, Kansas at 12:35 AM MST. Key Impacts: extreme cold, frostbite.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed towards the CWA late in the afternoon tightening the gradient ahead of it with strong downsloping winds from the southwest gusting up to 50-65 mph for southwestern counties in lee of the deepening surface low over eastern Colorado. Points to the north and east gusted up to 40-50 mph but below high wind warning criteria. Behind the cold front northwest winds gusted up to 40-50 mph in these areas for another hour or two into the evening hours after sunset before subsiding to 25-35 mph by midnight and less than 15-20 mph by sunrise.

The exact number of associated injuries is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought status in February for Northwest Kansas changed little.  The worst drought conditions continued to be between the Highway 25 and 83 corridors where D4 Exceptional Drought was located.  However this drought category did shift slightly south during the latter part of the month.  Otherwise most counties were in D3 Extreme Drought, with a smaller area in D2 Severe Drought (along the CO border).  Precipitation ranged from a quarter inch to two inches, with the highest amounts occurring in Rawlins and Thomas counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation for the month ranged from half an inch to one inch.  This was near normal to an inch above normal.  Normal precipitation amounts ranged from a quarter inch to an inch.

Amplified by frontogenic lift within the surface boundary and ample low-level moisture, snow continued through the afternoon hours before ending and moving northeast towards sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 6 inches of snow at Lakin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday, Jan. 18, 2023, a significant winter storm impacted the NWS Hastings coverage area, bringing heavy snow accumulations to many places along with some freezing rain. Much of the area saw snowfall accumulations of at least 6-10 inches with the highest totals of 15-17��� occurring in portions of south central Nebraska. For north central Kansas, precipitation began as freezing rain and sleet, with overall light icing/limited impacts. Mixed precipitation transitioned to snow from west to east throughout the day. While the highest snow rates (2-3��� per hour) remained further north in Nebraska, at least brief periods of moderate snow occurred near the NE/KS state line. Snow began to let up after sunset on the 18th, and accumulations came to an end for most locations by sunrise on Thursday the 19th.|While lingering falling snow ended early on the 19th, impacts from snowfall accumulations across the area led to slick roadways into the daytime hours on the 19th. Additional impacts included school, civic, business, and church closings on both Wednesday (the 18th) and Thursday (the 19th), though again, the most severe impacts remained further north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 6 inches across the county. A few of the highest amounts included the following: 4 inches, measured by the NWS Cooperative Observer located in Logan; 3.3 inches, measured by a CoCoRaHS observer located six miles east of Phillipsburg; 2 inches, measured by the NWS Cooperative Observer located in Kirwin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought several inches of snow to north central Kansas throughout the day on Saturday, January 21st. An upper level disturbance tracked along the Kansas and Oklahoma border during this event, which meant all of north central Kansas was on the cold, northern side of the system. A broad area of snow developed during the early morning hours of the 21st and light to moderate snow rates continued through the entire day. Snowfall began to taper off during the evening hours. Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the entire area and a Winter Weather Advisory was in effect. Fortunately, wind speeds remained light with this system, so widespread blowing and drifting snow was not observed. Impacts were typical for a storm system of this nature, such as slick roads, but the fact it occurred on a weekend likely led to more limited impacts to schools and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout January 2023, portions of four of the six North Central Kansas counties continued to be assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the 12th consecutive month that D2 plagued parts of the local area. Although a lingering, longer-term drought situation remained firmly intact, January offered some encouraging precipitation trends with widespread above to well-above normal totals (see below for more Jan. precipitation details). However, despite this above normal precipitation, there was actually no drought category change whatsoever within the local area. More specifically, the entire month featured D2 encompassing all of Rooks County, along with roughly the western two-thirds of Osborne County, the southwestern half of Phillips County and a portion of far southern Smith County. Elsewhere within the six-county domain, Moderate Drought (D1) persisted over the majority of Smith and Jewell counties, along with roughly the northeast half of Phillips County, the eastern third of Osborne County and a small portion of northwestern Mitchell County. Finally, the overall best-off areas, assigned Abnormally Dry (D0), remained over most of Mitchell along with southeastern Jewell counties.  ||Turning to January 2023 precipitation details, although actual precipitation amounts were not nearly enough to put a significant dent in drought categories (due to the winter months being the overall-driest months of the year on average), January nonetheless featured the most widespread coverage of above normal monthly precipitation within North Central Kansas in nearly two years...since March 2021. In fact, not only did the ENTIRE area measure above normal precipitation, but slightly over half of it scored at least twice-normal amounts. More specifically, the majority of the six-county domain received 1.00-1.50 inches of precipitation, or 160-240 percent of normal. The overall-highest totals favored Phillips and Smith counties, while the overall-lowest amounts concentrated across Mitchell and southeastern Jewell counties. Many long-standing official NWS stations notched a Top-10 wettest January, including 2nd-wettest at Kirwin Dam, 3rd-wettest at Smith Center, 5th-wettest near Plainville and 8th-wettest in Natoma. Per over 20 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, some of the highest January tallies featured: 1.75 at Kirwin Dam, 1.56 six miles east of Phillipsburg and 1.53 four miles west-northwest of Plainville. On the drier (but still above normal) side of the ledger, some of the lower totals included: 0.96 near Hunter, 0.99 in Beloit and 1.07 at Glen Elder Lake.

A storm system brought several inches of snow to north central Kansas throughout the day on Saturday, January 21st. An upper level disturbance tracked along the Kansas and Oklahoma border during this event, which meant all of north central Kansas was on the cold, northern side of the system. A broad area of snow developed during the early morning hours of the 21st and light to moderate snow rates continued through the entire day. Snowfall began to taper off during the evening hours. Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the entire area and a Winter Weather Advisory was in effect. Fortunately, wind speeds remained light with this system, so widespread blowing and drifting snow was not observed. Impacts were typical for a storm system of this nature, such as slick roads, but the fact it occurred on a weekend likely led to more limited impacts to schools and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southwest upper-level low made its way south of the CWA putting a good bullseye across southwest Kansas for snowfall. Ahead of the frontal and around the occlusion, many reports of 2-10 inches. Amplified by frontogenic lift within the surface boundary and ample low-level moisture, snow continued through the afternoon hours before ending and moving northeast towards sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 6.5 inches of snow at Healy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought several inches of snow to north central Kansas throughout the day on Saturday, January 21st. An upper level disturbance tracked along the Kansas and Oklahoma border during this event, which meant all of north central Kansas was on the cold, northern side of the system. A broad area of snow developed during the early morning hours of the 21st and light to moderate snow rates continued through the entire day. Snowfall began to taper off during the evening hours. Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the entire area and a Winter Weather Advisory was in effect. Fortunately, wind speeds remained light with this system, so widespread blowing and drifting snow was not observed. Impacts were typical for a storm system of this nature, such as slick roads, but the fact it occurred on a weekend likely led to more limited impacts to schools and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches were reported across the county.

The arctic cold front pushed through early in the morning with a strong shortwave out of the southwest from the upper level low near the four-corner region. This helped to sustain ample low-level moisture to produce snow showers from west to east throughout the late morning to evening timeframe tapering off overnight into Thursday to the east and northeast. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 5 inches, higher further north and west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported 2 inches of snow in Hugoton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong gradient across the southern half of the CWA with a leeside trough over eastern Colorado that deepened through the morning and into the afternoon hours. Aided by downsloping component to the wind flow, wind speeds out of the south to southwest gusted up to 50-60 mph into the evening hours ahead of an approaching cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought status in February for Northwest Kansas changed little.  The worst drought conditions continued to be between the Highway 25 and 83 corridors where D4 Exceptional Drought was located.  However this drought category did shift slightly south during the latter part of the month.  Otherwise most counties were in D3 Extreme Drought, with a smaller area in D2 Severe Drought (along the CO border).  Precipitation ranged from a quarter inch to two inches, with the highest amounts occurring in Rawlins and Thomas counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation for the month ranged from half an inch to one and a half inches.  This was half an inch to an inch above normal.  Normal precipitation amounts ranged from a quarter inch to half an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind the pre-frontal trough that turned winds out of the northwest, a stinger jet helped to aid in high wind criteria winds for some southwestern counties of the CWA. Aiding by peak heating of the early afternoon that mixed the boundary layer winds to the surface, wind speeds of 60-70 mph were had with a rogue 81 mph as well.

The arctic cold front pushed through early in the morning with a strong shortwave out of the southwest from the upper level low near the four-corner region. This helped to sustain ample low-level moisture to produce snow showers from west to east throughout the late morning to evening timeframe tapering off overnight into Thursday to the east and northeast. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 5 inches, higher further north and west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported 2.5 inches of snow in Syracuse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions began the month but eased to Severe (D2) Drought conditions by the end of February. Despite some improvement, drought conditions continued over most of southeastern Kansas during the month with limited rainfall through the month. Across southeastern Kansas D2 (Severe Drought) persisted. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month provided some limited help for mainly the northeastern half of the county. However, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of February. The US Drought Monitor indicated the far southwestern portion of the county as remaining in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of county in Moderate (D1) Drought or Abnormally Dry (D0). Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong gradient across the southern half of the CWA with a leeside trough over eastern Colorado that deepened through the morning and into the afternoon hours. Aided by downsloping component to the wind flow, wind speeds out of the south to southwest gusted up to 50-60 mph into the evening hours ahead of an approaching cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of April 19th, a cold front passed through Northwestern Kansas that produced damaging wind gusts up to 70 mph in the region. Blowing dust was also reported in the region due to these high winds that reduced visibilities down to near zero in some locations. An isolated thunderstorm also developed within the region due to the cold front passage that yielded hail up to a penny in size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility as low as near-zero statute miles reported within Sherman County due to high winds causing blowing dust. The lowest visibility of near-zero statute miles was reported 13 miles north of Edson, KS with an estimated 70 mph wind gust. Time range estimated using other local storm reports and KGLD ASOS observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, a strong supercell developed over Northeast Colorado that moved into Northwest Kansas, specifically Sherman County, during the evening hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of hail between pea to quarter in size over a duration of five minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest Kansas continued to see improvements with the drought.  Nearly all of the D2 Severe Drought area had shifted east.  However, three counties continued to have D2 to D3 Extreme Drought conditions.  Precipitation ranged from an inch and a half to eight inches, with some of the highest amounts falling in Norton County.  Normal precipitation ranges from two to three inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of February 26th, high winds were observed in Northwestern Kansas due to a passing cold front. The highest non-thunderstorm wind gust recorded in this region was 70 mph. These high winds allowed for localized blowing blowing dust that reduced visibilities down to a reported 0.25 statute miles. While there were hazardous traveling conditions within the region as a result, no injuries or damage were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm wind gusts up over 58 mph were recorded within the county. The highest wind gust of 70 mph was recorded 2 miles west-northwest of Sharon Springs, KS. Blowing dust was also reported within the county that reduced visibility down to between 0.5 and 0.25 statute miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north-south oriented snow squall developed off the Palmer Divide to extend across portions of East-Central Colorado moving east-northeast into Sherman, Wallace, and Greeley counties in Northwest Kansas. The snow squall created visibility reductions ranging as low as half a mile, which mainly impacted travel across I-70 and US-40. Snow reports ranged from three tenths to one inch of snow across the three counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. A band of heavy snowfall extending across portions of East-Central Colorado moved east-northeast across the Kansas border and Wallace County over a span of roughly one hour. The snow squall was dissipating as it moved over the county, creating visibility reductions as low as three-quarter to one-half mile that mainly impacted travel across US Highway 40. Snow amounts up to one inch were estimated. Key impacts: visibility reductions, hazardous travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north-south oriented snow squall developed off the Palmer Divide to extend across portions of East-Central Colorado moving east-northeast into Sherman, Wallace, and Greeley counties in Northwest Kansas. The snow squall created visibility reductions ranging as low as half a mile, which mainly impacted travel across I-70 and US-40. Snow reports ranged from three tenths to one inch of snow across the three counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. A band of heavy snowfall extending across portions of East-Central Colorado moved east-northeast across the Kansas border and Sherman County over a span of 45 minutes. The snow squall was dissipating as it moved over Sherman County, creating visibility reductions as low as a quarter mile in the western part of the county along I-70. Key impacts: visibility reductions, hazardous travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) to Severe (D2) Drought conditions continued with little changes fron December. Drought conditions continued over most of southeastern Kansas during the month with limited rainfall through the month. Across southeastern Kansas, D3 (Extreme Drought) to D2 (Severe Drought) persisted. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall during the months provided little improvement across the county, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of January. The US Drought Monitor indicated the western third of the county as being in Extreme (D3) Drought with the remainder of county in Severe (D2) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system swept through northwestern Kansas in the early hours of the 31st. The following pressure rise behind the low pressure system resulted in high winds. The Burlington, Colorado airport reported a 21 millibar pressure rise over 12 hours. The highest recorded gust in northwestern Kansas during this event was in Logan county with a gust of 63 mph. Localized blowing dust also occurred in Thomas county, reducing visibility to 1/4 mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple gusts of over 58 mph were recorded with the maximum gust of 61 mph recorded at the Goodland, Kansas ASOS at 3:06 PM. At 3:40 PM an off-duty NWS employee reported a 60 mph gust with blowing dust reducing visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and freezing fog occurred within Northwestern Kansas on the evening of March 8th going into the following morning of March 9th. The resulting icy roads and reduced visibilities down to 3/4 of a mile created hazardous driving conditions that led to vehicular accidents within the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and freezing fog was reported within Logan county creating low visibilities down to 3/4 of a mile and icy roads. Due to these hazardous driving conditions, an accident involving a semitruck occurred on I-70 at exit 76 just east of Oakley that caused all westbound lanes to be closed. No injuries were reported. Key impacts: hazardous traveling conditions, road closures, vehicular accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system led to isolated to scattered severe, non-thunderstorm wind gusts from March 30th into March 31st. This gust occurred on the warm side of the system and was out of the south. Most wind gusts during this afternoon were in the 40 to 55 MPH range. Humidity values were generally above 40 percent, which kept the fire weather threat from reaching critical levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located one mile south-southwest of Stockton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located four  miles north-northeast of Webster State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved southeast across much of north central and east central Kansas during the day on May 9th.  There were numerous reports of large hail and sporadic wind damage reports along with a few wind gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Storm chasers also documented a small tornado near Miltonvale.  That storm went on to produce another mainly rain wrapped tornado with minor tree damage in southwest Clay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser had photos of a small tornado that was on the ground for approximately 2 minutes just WSW of Miltonvale.  No damage was noted or reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone supercell thunderstorm moved from south central Kansas into central Kansas during the afternoon and evening of April 19th, 2023.  The cyclic storm produced very large hail up to 4 inches in diameter and numerous tornadoes across Chase county, Kansas.   Eight tornadoes formed from the supercell with the strongest producing EF2 damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the seventh tornado of the event. Damage was limited to trees and siding of homes.

The cyclic storm produced very large hail up to 4 inches in diameter and numerous tornadoes across Chase county, Kansas.   Eight tornadoes formed from the supercell with the strongest producing EF2 damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fourth tornado had a peak width of a half mile wide and was rated EF2. It caused the metal posts of an outbuilding to be twisted and scattered leaving the structure leaning. There was total destruction to a couple of outbuildings as well as significant tree damage.

These storms also produced small hail up to the size of peas in the city of Oakley, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a slow moving cold front during the evening hours of April 4th, 2023.  The thunderstorms zippered down from the northeast and produced numerous quarter size hail reports and a few half dollar size hail reports.  Some damaging wind reports also occurred over southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail cracked a car windshield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions developed across portions southeastern Kansas through the end of April. Despite some rainfall, drought conditions deepened over most of southeastern Kansas during the month. Across southeastern Kansas Extreme (D3) drought developed as rainfall remained lacking. Severe (D2) Drought persisted and expanded slightly with Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) around the Severe Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month provided limited help for portions of the county mainly in the south and southeastern areas. However, drought conditions remained across the region through the month of April. The US Drought Monitor indicated the northwestern portion of the county as remaining in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought or Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Early spring crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone supercell thunderstorm moved from south central Kansas into central Kansas during the afternoon and evening of April 19th, 2023.  The cyclic storm produced very large hail up to 4 inches in diameter and numerous tornadoes across Chase county, Kansas.   Eight tornadoes formed from the supercell with the strongest producing EF2 damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the first tornado which a storm survey revealed that an outbuilding was thrown 250 yards. Additional outbuildings were collapsed on site. The roof of a silo was removed from the top, and walls at the base were pushed in. This was rated an EF1 tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second tornado of the event was found following assessment of drone footage courtesy of Chase County Sheriff. This brief tornado damaged two farm outbuildings with parts of the roof torn off and was rated an EF0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed across north central Kansas during the morning hours of May 9th, 2023.  This complex of storms moved east-southeast across central and southeast Kansas, producing damaging wind gusts of 65 to 75 mph and large hail of 1.5 to 2 inches in diameter.  Additional severe storms developed behind the complex of storms during the late afternoon and evening hours and moved across south central Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 5-9 p.m. CDT on this Friday late afternoon-evening, a smattering of strong to marginally-severe thunderstorms streamed southwest-to-northeast through Jewell and Mitchell counties in the eastern part of this North Central Kansas area. These storms yielded a handful of reports of small hail (including up to dime size around Mankato), but just one report of severe-criteria hail (quarter size) east of Webber shortly after 6:30 p.m. CDT. ||This fairly high-based convection initiated along a well-defined cold front pushing slowly southward through the region, with this front situated from southwestern Kansas to far east-central Nebraska by late afternoon. The first cell of the day erupted over the heart of Jewell County around 5 p.m. CDT, dropping small hail in Mankato before departing northward into Nebraska. Around 6:30 p.m. CDT, more storms developed over northeastern Jewell County, producing the quarter size hail near Webber before also heading up into Nebraska. To the south in Mitchell County, there were no ground-truth storm reports, but a few storms sported radar indications of small hail/and or gusty winds, including one that brushed the far southeast edges of the county mainly between 7:30-8:30 p.m. CDT...shortly before all strong convection departed east of the local area for the night. In the big picture of the mid-upper levels, the Central Plains resided just downstream from a large-scale trough approaching from the Northern/Central Rockies. As storms developed over North Central Kansas, the mesoscale environment featured only 500-1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE, but strong effective deep-layer wind shear of 40-50 knots. At the surface, moisture was seasonably-scant, with dewpoints only topping out around 50 (F). Temperature-wise, the aforementioned cold front traversing the region promoted a healthy gradient across northern Kansas, with 6 p.m. readings ranging from only 53 degrees in Goodland, to 83 degrees in Concordia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After another month of below normal precipitation the severe drought across eastern Kansas persisted through the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While some areas saw some average or above average rain through July, most of the area across eastern Kansas saw the severe drought conditions continue through the month of July. As of now, widespread drought impacts have not been reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon-evening of the 19th, this six-county North Central Kansas area remained safely to the west of a zone of severe thunderstorms that raked across parts of south central and eastern Kansas. However, the overnight hours turned more active as a cluster of slightly-elevated, strong to severe storms swept across primarily Osborne, Mitchell, Smith and Jewell counties between 12-2 a.m. CDT on Thursday the 20th, prompting a few reported instances of large hail up to tennis ball size and unofficially-measured winds up to 67 MPH. Storms first erupted over the Rooks-Osborne County line and up into Smith County between 12:00-12:30 a.m. CDT. The southern-most cell quickly became severe, prompting accounts of ping pong ball to tennis ball size hail in far southern Osborne County and also a report of golf ball size stones near Hunter in Mitchell County. By 1 a.m. CDT, another storm over Jewell County reached severe levels, dropping quarter size hail in Mankato. As for winds, several area mesonet clocked primarily sub-severe gusts in the 45-55 MPH range. However, one unofficial personal weather station on the north side of Beloit registered gusts as high as 67 MPH (there were no known reports of wind damage). By 2 a.m. CDT, the back edge of what was now a small-scale quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) had departed North Central Kansas to the east-northeast, ending any local severe storm threat for the night. ||Turning to the meteorological situation, in short, this round of strong to severe storms was instigated by stout forcing/lift impinging upon a slightly-elevated frontal zone rooted around 850 millibars...positioned just to the cool side of a sharp surface front slicing southeastward across the region. Going into more detail, and starting with the bigger picture of the mid-upper levels, broad and seasonably-strong southwesterly flow overspread the Central Plains on the 19th, downstream from a large-scale trough/closed low centered over the MT/ND border region. At the surface, an initially slow-moving front stalled out slightly east of North Central Kansas during the afternoon-evening, sparing the local area from the swath of severe storms that impacted south central/eastern KS. However, as midnight loomed, a shortwave trough approached the area from the Central High Plains. At the surface, this upper disturbance caused low pressure to strengthen/consolidate over southwest KS by late evening (central pressure around 998 millibars). To the north-northwest of this low, a reinforcing cold front dove southeastward across North Central Kansas during the evening, while just to the east of the area, the initial boundary that fired the afternoon convection began retreating northwestward a bit, effectively tightening up/sharpening a low level frontal zone aligned south-southwest to north-northeast through the heart of Kansas. As the mid level wave intercepted the west edge of the instability axis rooted within the sharp elevated frontal zone based around 850 millibars, the North Central Kansas storms rapidly flared up during the midnight (CDT) hour. Despite the local area residing just behind the surface front, the mesoscale environment was primed for slightly-elevated severe convection, as although most-unstable CAPE values were somewhat modest at only 500-1000 J/kg, effective deep-layer wind shear was quite vigorous at 50-60 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball to tennis ball size hail was reported.

During the afternoon hours, scattered storms developed over Northwest Kansas as a warm front moved north. These storms produced hail ranging from pea to golf ball in size and a brief tornado near the town of Selden. The largest hail fell near the towns of Selden, Levant, and Edson. Heavy rain with amounts up to  five inches produced flash flooding over portions of Thomas and Sheridan counties. These storms also produced strong winds with gusts up to 81 mph reported. The peak gust occurred just south of Selden, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado reported northwest of Selden, KS. Tornado remained over open fields along the Sheridan and Decatur County borders. No damage was reported as a result of this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved southeast across much of north central and east central Kansas during the day on May 9th.  There were numerous reports of large hail and sporadic wind damage reports along with a few wind gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Storm chasers also documented a small tornado near Miltonvale.  That storm went on to produce another mainly rain wrapped tornado with minor tree damage in southwest Clay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 11 inch diameter tree limb down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During April 2023, at least portions of five of the six North Central Kansas counties continued to be (or were newly) assigned at least Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the 15th consecutive month that D2 plagued parts of the local area. However, of greater concern during the month was the introduction of both Extreme Drought (D3) and worst-off Exceptional Drought (D4) into mainly southwestern parts of the area. This marked the first appearance of D3 anywhere within North Central Kansas in nearly 9 years (since June 2014) and the first instance of D4 in the area in over 9.5 years (since August 2013). It is little surprise that the drought situation went further downhill during the month, given that a very dry March was immediately followed by an exceptionally dry April (see below for more April precipitation details). Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics during the month, things opened with D2 covering all of Rooks County, along with roughly the western two-thirds of Osborne County, the southwestern half of Phillips County and a narrow strip of southern Smith County. Elsewhere within the six-county domain, Moderate Drought (D1) was present to start April in nearly all other places, except for a lingering zone of overall-best-off Abnormally Dry D0 within eastern Mitchell County. However, degradation got underway already on the April 11th USDM issuance, as D3 was introduced to southern portions of Rooks/Osborne counties, D2 expanded eastward into western Mitchell County, and eastern Mitchell County went from D0 to D1. Through the rest of the month, the entire D1 area and most of the D2 area remained unchanged, but the D3 zone expanded northward and D4 was introduced to far southwest locales. More specifically, by the April 25th USDM, D4 was present across much of the southern two-thirds of Rooks County along with southwestern Osborne County, and a narrow buffer zone of D3 extended northward into more of Rooks, Osborne, southwestern Phillips and extreme southwestern Mitchell counties. At month's end, only Jewell County remained void of D2-or-worse. ||Turning to April 2023 precipitation details, it was overall bad news, with all of North Central Kansas registering between moderately and tremendously-below normal precipitation. More specifically, the majority of the six-county domain totaled between 0.15-0.90 inch, or roughly 5-40 percent of normal. In fact, around 95 percent of the area measured less than one-half of monthly normals, and roughly 85% of it registered less than ONE-FOURTH of normal! Some official, long-term NWS stations bemoaned a Top-5 driest April on record, including Plainville 4WNW (0.06...THE DRIEST on record out of 114) and Beloit (0.43...2nd-driest out of 115). Per around 20 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, some of the most dire April tallies included: 0.06 four miles west north-west of Plainville, 0.08 ten miles west-northwest of Stockton, 0.10 at Webster Dam and 0.14 in Cawker City. On the slightly more fortunate (but still well below normal side of things), a few of the highest monthly totals featured: 1.24 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit, 0.92 at Lovewell Dam and 0.87 in Natoma.

During April 2023, at least portions of five of the six North Central Kansas counties continued to be (or were newly) assigned at least Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the 15th consecutive month that D2 plagued parts of the local area. However, of greater concern during the month was the introduction of both Extreme Drought (D3) and worst-off Exceptional Drought (D4) into mainly southwestern parts of the area. This marked the first appearance of D3 anywhere within North Central Kansas in nearly 9 years (since June 2014) and the first instance of D4 in the area in over 9.5 years (since August 2013). It is little surprise that the drought situation went further downhill during the month, given that a very dry March was immediately followed by an exceptionally dry April (see below for more April precipitation details). Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics during the month, things opened with D2 covering all of Rooks County, along with roughly the western two-thirds of Osborne County, the southwestern half of Phillips County and a narrow strip of southern Smith County. Elsewhere within the six-county domain, Moderate Drought (D1) was present to start April in nearly all other places, except for a lingering zone of overall-best-off Abnormally Dry D0 within eastern Mitchell County. However, degradation got underway already on the April 11th USDM issuance, as D3 was introduced to southern portions of Rooks/Osborne counties, D2 expanded eastward into western Mitchell County, and eastern Mitchell County went from D0 to D1. During the afternoon hours, scattered storms developed over Northwest Kansas as a warm front moved north. These storms produced hail ranging from pea to golf ball in size and a brief tornado near the town of Selden. The largest hail fell near the towns of Selden, Levant, and Edson. Heavy rain with amounts up to  five inches produced flash flooding over portions of Thomas and Sheridan counties. These storms also produced strong winds with gusts up to 81 mph reported. The peak gust occurred just south of Selden, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny sized hail report received via mPING.

Throughout February 2023, at least portions of four of the six North Central Kansas counties continued to be assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the 13th consecutive month that D2 plagued parts of the local area. Although the lingering, longer-term drought situation remained firmly intact, at least parts of the area saw encouraging trends for the second straight month, with roughly two-thirds of the area picking up near-to-slightly above normal precipitation (see below for more Feb. precipitation details). Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics during February, there was actually no change whatsoever across North Central Kansas over the course of the month. The month opened and closed with D2 afflicting all of Rooks County, along with roughly the western two-thirds of Osborne County, the southwestern half of Phillips County and a portion of far southern Smith County. Elsewhere within the six-county domain, Moderate Drought (D1) persisted over the majority of Smith and Jewell counties, along with roughly the northeast half of Phillips County, the eastern third of Osborne County and a small portion of northwestern Mitchell County. Finally, the overall best-off areas, assigned Abnormally Dry (D0), lingered over most of Mitchell along with southeastern Jewell counties.||Turning to February 2023 precipitation details, although total amounts were not nearly enough to put a significant dent in drought categories (due to the winter months being the overall-driest months of the year on average), February nonetheless carried over some promising trends from January for the roughly two-thirds of the area that received near-to-slightly-above normal precipitation. More specifically, most areas along and north of a line from Plainville-Osborne-Mankato totaled somewhere between 0.65-1.00 inches, or roughly 80-120 percent of normal. The totals in these areas were bolstered in large part by a somewhat significant mid-month winter storm that dumped 3-7 of snow. Unfortunately, below normal monthly amounts prevailed in the southeastern reaches of the local area, particularly within much of Mitchell, southern Osborne and southeastern Rooks Counties, where totals were mainly in the 0.25-0.50 range at best. Per around 20 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, some of the highest February tallies featured: 1.12 at Kirwin Dam, 1.10 in Phillipsburg and 1.04 four miles west-northwest of Plainville. On the less fortunate side of the precipitation ledger, some of the lowest February totals included: 0.26 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit, 0.30 in Natoma and 0.45 near Hunter.

A line of thunderstorms moved southeast across much of north central and east central Kansas during the day on May 9th.  There were numerous reports of large hail and sporadic wind damage reports along with a few wind gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Storm chasers also documented a small tornado near Miltonvale.  That storm went on to produce another mainly rain wrapped tornado with minor tree damage in southwest Clay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail reported.

Between the afternoon of Wednesday the 15th and mid-morning of Thursday the 16th, this six-county North Central Kansas area experienced a moderately-strong winter storm that deposited widespread snow totals of mainly 3-7 inches, with limited higher/lower exceptions. Per a mix of official observations and NOHRSC analysis, the highest concentration of 6+ amounts focused within parts of Rooks/Phillips counties, along with perhaps the northern reaches of Smith/Jewell counties. On the lower end of the spectrum, most of Mitchell and southern Jewell counties saw no more than 2 (including merely 0.3 in Beloit), due to this portion of the area being most impacted by the mid-level dry slot of the responsible storm system. Though not overly-strong by Central Plains standards, north winds were certainly a factor as well, with sustained speeds commonly 20-25 MPH/gusts 30-35 MPH prompting modest blowing/drifting. According to around 20 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, some of the very-highest totals featured: 8.8 four miles west-northwest of Plainville, 6.0 at Kirwin Dam and also six miles east of Phillipsburg, 5.5 ten miles west-northwest of Stockton and 5.0 in Logan. Although this snow contained a beneficial dose of much-needed liquid moisture content, it was also a somewhat dry in nature given that temperatures were fairly cold, averaging in the teens (F). This blanket of fresh snow did not stick around very long, as a steady warm-up into the 40s-50s between the 17th-19th spurred a steady melt-off.||Taking a closer look at the timing and meteorological background behind this event, the first bands of light snow lifted across the area from south-to-north mainly between 12-3 p.m. on the 15th, laying down a thin coating on roads for the evening commute. However, the main event clearly unfolded under the cover of darkness, with the heaviest snow bands slowly traversing North Central Kansas from west-to-east between 9 p.m. and 7 a.m. on the 16th, with most areas catching a break in snow intensity prior to the main morning commute. In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was a large-scale, positively-tilted trough slowly shifting across the central United States. In the mid-levels (particularly evident at 700 millibars), a compact, closed low intensified directly overhead during the night as it lifted northeast out of eastern Colorado. Strong frontogenetical forcing under this low subsequently prompted the development of a relatively narrow/focused mesoscale snow band, with amounts of 6+ inches within/near the local area largely confined to a west-southwest to east-northeast swath only about 40 miles wide. At the surface, North Central Kansas resided well to the north of a sharp frontal zone stretched from the Southern Plains into the Ohio Valley, with the seasonably-cold airmass and gusty north winds driven by a healthy pressure gradient between a 1003 millibar low that tracked from Oklahoma into Arkansas during the night and a sprawling 1033 millibar high centered over the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow amounts were estimated to have generally ranged from 2 to 5 inches across the county. Highest reported amounts included: 4 inches, measured by emergency management in Osborne; 3 inches, measured by the NWS Cooperative Observer in Natoma and also by a CoCoRaHS observer seven miles north-northeast of Natoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed across north central Kansas during the morning hours of May 9th, 2023.  This complex of storms moved east-southeast across central and southeast Kansas, producing damaging wind gusts of 65 to 75 mph and large hail of 1.5 to 2 inches in diameter.  Additional severe storms developed behind the complex of storms during the late afternoon and evening hours and moved across south central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down at the 2300 block of south Victoria in Wichita. The lines were down in a yard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown onto a house in the 1300 block of north Forrest and the 400 block of north Erie. A person was reported trapped by the trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 14, 2023 a strong storm formed in northeast Kansas and eventually transformed into a strong squall line that impacted far eastern Kansas and western Missouri. This storm left a swath of wind damage mainly through the heart of the Kansas City metro area in the form of widespread tree damage. Several structures were impacted by falling trees as the storm moved through the city, and into central Missouri. The highest reported wind gust came from western Johnson County where a personal weather station recorded 89 mph wind just west of Lenexa. Numerous wind reports, both estimated and measured were over 70 mph. At one point nearly 200,000 people across the Kansas City metro lost power. As of the next day, roughly 60,000 Kansas Citians were still without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 60 mph winds just west of Jarbalo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon to early evening hours, scattered storms developed over Northeast Colorado and continued east across Northwest Kansas. These storms produced up to lime sized hail in Decatur and Sherman Counties along with numerous severe wind gusts ranging from 58 to 65 mph. The strongest wind gust was observed near Selden, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the afternoon of Wednesday the 15th and mid-morning of Thursday the 16th, this six-county North Central Kansas area experienced a moderately-strong winter storm that deposited widespread snow totals of mainly 3-7 inches, with limited higher/lower exceptions. Per a mix of official observations and NOHRSC analysis, the highest concentration of 6+ amounts focused within parts of Rooks/Phillips counties, along with perhaps the northern reaches of Smith/Jewell counties. On the lower end of the spectrum, most of Mitchell and southern Jewell counties saw no more than 2 (including merely 0.3 in Beloit), due to this portion of the area being most impacted by the mid-level dry slot of the responsible storm system. Though not overly-strong by Central Plains standards, north winds were certainly a factor as well, with sustained speeds commonly 20-25 MPH/gusts 30-35 MPH prompting modest blowing/drifting. According to around 20 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, some of the very-highest totals featured: 8.8 four miles west-northwest of Plainville, 6.0 at Kirwin Dam and also six miles east of Phillipsburg, 5.5 ten miles west-northwest of Stockton and 5.0 in Logan. Although this snow contained a beneficial dose of much-needed liquid moisture content, it was also a somewhat dry in nature given that temperatures were fairly cold, averaging in the teens (F). This blanket of fresh snow did not stick around very long, as a steady warm-up into the 40s-50s between the 17th-19th spurred a steady melt-off.||Taking a closer look at the timing and meteorological background behind this event, the first bands of light snow lifted across the area from south-to-north mainly between 12-3 p.m. on the 15th, laying down a thin coating on roads for the evening commute. However, the main event clearly unfolded under the cover of darkness, with the heaviest snow bands slowly traversing North Central Kansas from west-to-east between 9 p.m. and 7 a.m. on the 16th, with most areas catching a break in snow intensity prior to the main morning commute. In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was a large-scale, positively-tilted trough slowly shifting across the central United States. In the mid-levels (particularly evident at 700 millibars), a compact, closed low intensified directly overhead during the night as it lifted northeast out of eastern Colorado. Strong frontogenetical forcing under this low subsequently prompted the development of a relatively narrow/focused mesoscale snow band, with amounts of 6+ inches within/near the local area largely confined to a west-southwest to east-northeast swath only about 40 miles wide. At the surface, North Central Kansas resided well to the north of a sharp frontal zone stretched from the Southern Plains into the Ohio Valley, with the seasonably-cold airmass and gusty north winds driven by a healthy pressure gradient between a 1003 millibar low that tracked from Oklahoma into Arkansas during the night and a sprawling 1033 millibar high centered over the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow amounts were estimated to have generally ranged from 4 to 9 inches across the county. Highest reported amounts included: 8.8 inches, measured by the NWS Cooperative Observer four miles west-northwest of Plainville; 5.5 inches, measured by a CoCoRaHS observer seven miles north of Webster State Park; 4.0 inches, measured by the NWS Cooperative Observer at Webster Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon a thunderstorm moved southeast out of Nebraska into Northwest Kansas.  The thunderstorm had produced severe wind gusts near Haigler, and continued to produce them as it moved southeast.  A peak gust of 67 MPH was measured near St. Francis.  Hail up to quarter size was reported in St. Francis from this storm.  Almost one and a half inches of rainfall was reported near St. Francis from this storm.  The high rainfall rate caused water to flow down the streets of St. Francis, becoming curb deep at the bottom of the hill in town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating pea to nickel sized hail with torrential rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed over southeast Kansas during the early morning of the 9th as a low level jet interacted with a stalled front along the northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma border. These storms dissipated during the late morning, but left another outflow boundary that interacted with an approaching storm complex to produce another round of strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Wind gusts over 60 mph were reported in far southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved southeast across much of north central and east central Kansas during the day on May 9th.  There were numerous reports of large hail and sporadic wind damage reports along with a few wind gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Storm chasers also documented a small tornado near Miltonvale.  That storm went on to produce another mainly rain wrapped tornado with minor tree damage in southwest Clay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a dramatic improve in the drought in this part of the state due to the abundant rainfall. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts ranged from 6-10 inches for the month.  This was four to five inches above the normal amount of two to four inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed across north central Kansas during the morning hours of May 9th, 2023.  This complex of storms moved east-southeast across central and southeast Kansas, producing damaging wind gusts of 65 to 75 mph and large hail of 1.5 to 2 inches in diameter.  Additional severe storms developed behind the complex of storms during the late afternoon and evening hours and moved across south central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout February 2023, at least portions of four of the six North Central Kansas counties continued to be assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the 13th consecutive month that D2 plagued parts of the local area. Although the lingering, longer-term drought situation remained firmly intact, at least parts of the area saw encouraging trends for the second straight month, with roughly two-thirds of the area picking up near-to-slightly above normal precipitation (see below for more Feb. precipitation details). Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics during February, there was actually no change whatsoever across North Central Kansas over the course of the month. The month opened and closed with D2 afflicting all of Rooks County, along with roughly the western two-thirds of Osborne County, the southwestern half of Phillips County and a portion of far southern Smith County. Elsewhere within the six-county domain, Moderate Drought (D1) persisted over the majority of Smith and Jewell counties, along with roughly the northeast half of Phillips County, the eastern third of Osborne County and a small portion of northwestern Mitchell County. Finally, the overall best-off areas, assigned Abnormally Dry (D0), lingered over most of Mitchell along with southeastern Jewell counties.||Turning to February 2023 precipitation details, although total amounts were not nearly enough to put a significant dent in drought categories (due to the winter months being the overall-driest months of the year on average), February nonetheless carried over some promising trends from January for the roughly two-thirds of the area that received near-to-slightly-above normal precipitation. More specifically, most areas along and north of a line from Plainville-Osborne-Mankato totaled somewhere between 0.65-1.00 inches, or roughly 80-120 percent of normal. The totals in these areas were bolstered in large part by a somewhat significant mid-month winter storm that dumped 3-7 of snow. Unfortunately, below normal monthly amounts prevailed in the southeastern reaches of the local area, particularly within much of Mitchell, southern Osborne and southeastern Rooks Counties, where totals were mainly in the 0.25-0.50 range at best. Per around 20 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, some of the highest February tallies featured: 1.12 at Kirwin Dam, 1.10 in Phillipsburg and 1.04 four miles west-northwest of Plainville. On the less fortunate side of the precipitation ledger, some of the lowest February totals included: 0.26 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit, 0.30 in Natoma and 0.45 near Hunter.

A line of thunderstorms moved southeast across much of north central and east central Kansas during the day on May 9th.  There were numerous reports of large hail and sporadic wind damage reports along with a few wind gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Storm chasers also documented a small tornado near Miltonvale.  That storm went on to produce another mainly rain wrapped tornado with minor tree damage in southwest Clay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a dramatic improve in the drought in this part of the state due to the abundant rainfall.  The western edge of the D4 Exceptional Drought shifted eastward to a Decatur and Greeley County line.  Rainfall amounts ranged from 3-15 inches, with the highest amounts in Rawlins County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts ranged from four to six inches for the month.  This was one to five inches above the normal amount of two to three inches for the month.

Additional severe storms developed behind the complex of storms during the late afternoon and evening hours and moved across south central Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One tornado was documented that moved north out of Chase county into far southeast Morris County around 905 pm CDT.   The tornado did damage to trees and one home before ending around 3 miles south southwest of Dunlap, Kansas.  Other storms produced large hail and damaging winds.  A second line of severe storms developed after midnight during the early morning hours of April 20th and produced wind damage most concentrated in Clay County in the city of Clay Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Department Chief relayed damage reports from southeast side of Randolph damage to signs at intersection of Senn Road, highway 77 and Gardiner road along with minor tree limb damage.  Short path and sporadic sometimes very sparse damage appears to suggest any vortex was not on the ground continuously but aloft more often than not as it moved across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed over southeast Kansas during the early morning of the 9th as a low level jet interacted with a stalled front along the northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma border. These storms dissipated during the late morning, but left another outflow boundary that interacted with an approaching storm complex to produce another round of strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Wind gusts over 60 mph were reported in far southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was reported down on a home causing minor roof damage.

During the evening of May 28th, severe thunderstorms developed within Northwestern Kansas. These thunderstorms produced hail up to golf balls in size as well as torrential rainfall that resulted in flash flooding. Local law enforcement had to perform one water rescue due to the flood conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received of ping pong ball sized hail at location. Time is radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed along a dryline and moved slowly across parts of northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and evening of May 12th.  At least 5 tornadoes were documented from these storms.  A couple of the tornadoes did produce damage to structures in Jackson and Brown Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just north of Holton damaging a well constructed barn. Large portions of the roof were blown off and some of the walls were blown out from winds estimated to be up to 90 mph. The tornado skipped northeast across the county side and produced some tree damage as it moved towards Straight Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed along a dryline and moved slowly across parts of northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and evening of May 12th.  At least 5 tornadoes were documented from these storms.  A couple of the tornadoes did produce damage to structures in Jackson and Brown Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down north of Hamlin and moves north-northeast producing some tree damage and overturning a center pivot before hitting a homestead. Outbuildings were completely destroyed with winds estimated to be 112 mph. The home sustained damage to the siding and roof with the windows broken out. The tornado dissipated after hitting the homestead.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado was observed between Netawaka and Whiting around 7:38 PM. While photo evidence exists of the tornado kicking up dirt beneath its funnel, no damage was observed due to the brief and rural location. Exact location estimated based on photo and radar evidence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado that formed for around 30 seconds, in an inaccessible area near the Delaware River north of Whiting. Photo evidence exists from several observers of this tornado, but no damage was found do the brief and rural nature of this tornado. Exact location estimated based on photo and radar evidence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, scattered storms developed over Northwest Kansas as a warm front moved north. These storms produced hail ranging from pea to golf ball in size and a brief tornado near the town of Selden. The largest hail fell near the towns of Selden, Levant, and Edson. Heavy rain with amounts up to  five inches produced flash flooding over portions of Thomas and Sheridan counties. These storms also produced strong winds with gusts up to 81 mph reported. The peak gust occurred just south of Selden, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage to farm outbuildings, irrigation pivots, and street signs. Seems to be an RFD from an intensifying supercell that produced the tornado northwest of Selden, KS.

Additional severe storms developed behind the complex of storms during the late afternoon and evening hours and moved across south central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high impact storm system moved across Northwest Kansas. The storm system produced an environment that was favorable for any of the developing thunderstorms to produce a tornado, which led to multiple tornadoes occurring on this day.  The strongest tornado was rated an EF-1 which caused damage to the high school in Weskan.  ||Otherwise thunderstorms produced hail up to half dollar in size near Wallace, and wind gusts estimated up to 65 MPH near Campus.  The thunderstorms on this day were heavy rain producers, with rainfall amounts ranging from two to four inches.  This led to some towns having street flooding as the storms moved through.  One location near Weskan had flood damage a barn.||The repeated rounds of heavy rainfall over the Ludell area, combined with runoff being funneled into Beaver Creek from the area, caused flooding to occur at Ludell.  The flood waters also flooded the county road going east from Ludell that followed Beaver Creek to Herndon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was running over the county road between Ludell and Herndon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought conditions continued across southeastern Kansas through the end of March. Despite some rainfall, drought conditions continued over most of southeastern Kansas during the month. Across southeastern Kansas D2 (Severe Drought) persisted with Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) around the Severe Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month provided only limited help for mainly the northern half of the county. However, drought conditions deepened across the region through the month of March. The US Drought Monitor indicated the far southern portion of the county as remaining in Severe (D2) Drought with all but the far northeastern portion of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought or Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Early spring crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.
