EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout March 2023, at least portions of four of the six North Central Kansas counties continued to be assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the 14th consecutive month that D2 plagued parts of the local area. Unfortunately, following some encouraging recent precipitation trends during January-February, March ushered in renewed disappointment as the entire area registered WELL below normal precipitation (see below for more March precipitation details). Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics during March, there was overall-little change within North Central Kansas as a whole, including no change whatsoever to the D2 area. As a result, the month opened and closed with D2 afflicting all of Rooks County, along with roughly the western two-thirds of Osborne County, the southwestern half of Phillips County and a narrow strip of southern Smith County. Elsewhere within the six-county domain, Moderate Drought (D1) persisted within the majority of other locations throughout March, including most of Smith and Jewell counties, roughly the northeast half of Phillips County, the eastern third of Osborne County and a small portion of northwestern Mitchell County. On the first USDM release of the month (valid March 7th), D1 was expanded southeastward to include more of Mitchell County along with the remainder of Jewell County. Following this early-month degradation, the only remaining zone of overall-best-off Abnormally Dry (D0) within the area was confined to parts of eastern Mitchell County.||Turning to March 2023 precipitation details, the entirety of North Central Kansas tallied significantly below normal precipitation, just with some places worse off than others. The majority of the six-county area totaled between 0.20 and 0.40 inch, or roughly 15-30 percent of normal. In fact, not only did almost the entire area receive less than one-third of normal March precipitation, but around 75% of it received no more than one-fourth of normal. Although there were really no winners to speak of, if anything the overall-highest monthly amounts tended to favor Jewell, Mitchell and Smith counties. Per around 20 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, some of the highest March tallies featured only: 0.49 at Lovewell Dam, 0.44 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit and 0.41 near Hunter. On the very driest side of things, a few of the lowest March totals included: 0.09 in Natoma, 0.21 six miles east of Phillipsburg and 0.22 seven miles north-northeast of Natoma.

A high impact storm system moved across Northwest Kansas. The storm system produced an environment that was favorable for any of the developing thunderstorms to produce a tornado, which led to multiple tornadoes occurring on this day.  The strongest tornado was rated an EF-1 which caused damage to the high school in Weskan.  ||Otherwise thunderstorms produced hail up to half dollar in size near Wallace, and wind gusts estimated up to 65 MPH near Campus.  The thunderstorms on this day were heavy rain producers, with rainfall amounts ranging from two to four inches.  This led to some towns having street flooding as the storms moved through.  One location near Weskan had flood damage a barn.||The repeated rounds of heavy rainfall over the Ludell area, combined with runoff being funneled into Beaver Creek from the area, caused flooding to occur at Ludell.  The flood waters also flooded the county road going east from Ludell that followed Beaver Creek to Herndon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado west of McDonald reported by spotters and general public. Several pictures and videos received, including one video that made national media attention. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system tracked across the Plains on March 31st, 2023. South central and southeast Kansas reported wind gusts in the 55 to 70 mph range, which caused tree and structure damage. These winds made grass fires very difficult to control. Dry conditions with a recently tilled field combined with the strong winds resulted in dirt to be picked up in Labette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous homes had shingles blown off and several buildings had canopies blown off along Main Street in Sedan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high impact storm system moved across Northwest Kansas. The storm system produced an environment that was favorable for any of the developing thunderstorms to produce a tornado, which led to multiple tornadoes occurring on this day.  The strongest tornado was rated an EF-1 which caused damage to the high school in Weskan.  ||Otherwise thunderstorms produced hail up to half dollar in size near Wallace, and wind gusts estimated up to 65 MPH near Campus.  The thunderstorms on this day were heavy rain producers, with rainfall amounts ranging from two to four inches.  This led to some towns having street flooding as the storms moved through.  One location near Weskan had flood damage a barn.||The repeated rounds of heavy rainfall over the Ludell area, combined with runoff being funneled into Beaver Creek from the area, caused flooding to occur at Ludell.  The flood waters also flooded the county road going east from Ludell that followed Beaver Creek to Herndon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado south of McDonald reported by spotters and general public. Several pictures and videos received. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Middle to upper level winds were out of the west to southwest, and an upper level disturbance moved over the region from Colorado by the early evening hours. There was a weak surface low to the south with an inverted trough axis over portions of north central Kansas and south central Nebraska extending north of the low. This trough axis set up a frontal zone across Nebraska and Kansas. High based thunderstorms developed to the west of the area along a shortwave trough then moved eastward and encountered increased instability. Mixed layer CAPE was around 1000 to 2000 J/kg and deep layer wind shear was around 50 to 60 knots. There was a slight risk of severe storms from the Storm Prediction Center. ||Storms began developing to the west and northwest of the area during the afternoon hours. A cluster of storms moved in from the west and began impacting north central Kansas around 7 pm. This cluster of storms continued moving eastward during the evening hours and extended northward into portions of southern Nebraska. The cluster exited the area around 10:30 pm as it continued tracking eastward across Nebraska. There were several strong to severe wind gusts across north central Kansas and one report of golfball sized hail. One report of thunderstorm wind damage occurred in Mitchell County. The storms went on to produce a few severe wind gusts in Nuckolls and Thayer Counties in Nebraska as well as a wind damage report in Thayer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 62 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located four miles north-northeast of Webster State Park.

A cluster of storms moved in from the west and began impacting north central Kansas around 7 pm. This cluster of storms continued moving eastward during the evening hours and extended northward into portions of southern Nebraska. The cluster exited the area around 10:30 pm as it continued tracking eastward across Nebraska. There were several strong to severe wind gusts across north central Kansas and one report of golfball sized hail. One report of thunderstorm wind damage occurred in Mitchell County. The storms went on to produce a few severe wind gusts in Nuckolls and Thayer Counties in Nebraska as well as a wind damage report in Thayer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located eight miles south-southeast of Glade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During May 2023, drought further worsened across the vast majority of this six-county North Central Kansas area per the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM), putting the area under its overall-worst drought classification in a decade. By month's end, most of the domain was assigned at least Severe Drought (D2), marking the most widespread local coverage of D2-or-worse since April 2013. On a more recent note, this also marked the 16th consecutive month that D2 plagued at least some portion of the local area. However, of greater concern during May was a steady expansion of both Extreme Drought (D3) and worst-off Exceptional Drought (D4) as a gradually-worsening drought situation over the last few years ratcheted up another notch. By the time May closed, at least portions of all six local counties were afflicted with D3 and at least portions of five counties contained D4, marking the greatest local coverage of D4 since Feb. 2013. Although precipitation deficits during May were not as extreme as those during March-April, the ever-building dryness heading into the 2023 growing season made significant drought degradation an inevitability (see below for more May precipitation details). Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics during the month, the first two USDM issuances of the month featured widespread expansion of D2+D3. More specifically, the vast majority of the previously-better-off Moderate Drought (D1) zone within all of Jewell County along with much of Smith/Jewell counties was quickly converted to D2/D3, leaving only a lingering stripe of D1 across eastern Mitchell and extreme southeast Jewell counties. As for the worst-off D4 areas, although the first half of May saw a slight northward expansion across more of Rooks County, all local D4 remained confined to much of Rooks and southwestern Osborne counties. The second half of the month brought further increases in the areal coverage of D3/D4, with D3 expanding into northeastern Smith/northwestern Jewell counties and D4 ballooning to include much more of Osborne County along with small portions of southern Smith, far southwestern Jewell and far western Mitchell counties. At month's end, roughly 95 percent of the six-county area was assigned D2-or-worse, roughly two-thirds of the domain contained D3-or worse and around one-third of the area featured worst-off D4 (including the vast majority of Rooks/Osborne counties). ||Turning to May 2023 precipitation details, rainfall trends across the six counties as a whole were best described as moderately (but not extremely) dry. Totals were actually relatively variable across the area, although the majority of the domain tallied between 1.50-4.00 inches, or about 35-95 percent of normal. Unfortunately, around 95% of the area checked in at least slightly below normal, and more concerningly, nearly 40% of the area fared no better than one-third to one-half of normal. Per a combination of measured totals from around 20 NWS/CoCoRaHS observers along with AHPS precipitation analysis, the very-driest pocket in North Central Kansas was centered over east-central Osborne County, while the primary, tiny patches of perhaps slightly above normal rainfall resided over north central Phillips and far south central Rooks counties (unfortunately no ground-truth gauges in these particular places). One of the driest long-term NWS stations was Beloit, which registered its 12th-driest May on record (out of 114 available years) with only 1.36. More notably, Beloit endured its 4th-driest March-May (meteorological spring) on record with merely 2.17/26% of normal. Getting back to May-only data, a few of the lowest NWS/CoCoRaHS totals were: 1.36 in Beloit, 1.41 near Hunter, 1.51 near Osborne (KSU mesonet) and 1.62 at Cawker City. On the more fortunate (but no better than near to slightly below normal) side of things, a few of the highest monthly amounts featured: 4.12 in Plainville, 4.06 in Lebanon, 3.67 at Logan and 3.66 in Ionia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall amounts occurred across Northwest Kansas during the month.  This allowed for a slight reduction in the counties affected by the severe and exceptional drought, namely Thomas, Greeley, and Wichita counties.  Rainfall amounts in the counties still affected by severe or worse drought ranged from 3-11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts for the month ranged from four to eight inches.  This was two to six inches above the normal amount of two to three inches of rainfall for the month.

At the time, there have been minimal impacts from this drought.

At the time, there have been minimal impacts from this drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of June 10th, a broken line of thunderstorms moved across Northwestern Kansas. Severe thunderstorms occurred within this line which produced damaging strong wind gusts up to 70 mph within the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported an estimated 60 mph wind gust at location. Wind gusts rattled the house more than usual and caused rain to be blown through a deadbolt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of June 17th, 2023, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across the High Plains (eastern Colorado and the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles) and quickly progressed eastward through the late afternoon and evening hours. Storms entered portions of central and south-central Kansas around sunset and produced primarily damaging winds around 60 to 80 mph as they swept across the area. A couple storms ahead of the line also produced up to quarter sized hail before merging with the advancing line of storms from the west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The winds were measured to be 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early afternoon of the 16th, a system of thunderstorms formed in east central Colorado and progressed eastward into Kansas. The severe cells of this system remained about 30 miles south of I-70 until it was near the Oakley area, where it began a northeastward trend. Up to 2 inch diameter hail and a 67 mph wind gust was reported from this system. Multiple reports of 2 inches of rain were also reported with the core of this system which caused flooding to occur in Wallace county for the following days. Another round of heavy rain occurred on the 17th, which worsened the flooding that began on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wallace county dispatch received numerous reports of pea to quarter size hail throughout northern portions of the county, particularly about 10 miles north of Sharon Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Due to several weeks of relatively dry conditions moderate to severe drought was introduced to eastern Kansas by the US Drought Monitor. According to the Advanced Hydrologic Prediction Service page there was a monthly deficit of around 3 to 5 inches of rain, which led to the declaration of drought. At the time, there have been minimal impacts from this drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early afternoon of the 16th, a system of thunderstorms formed in east central Colorado and progressed eastward into Kansas. The severe cells of this system remained about 30 miles south of I-70 until it was near the Oakley area, where it began a northeastward trend. Up to 2 inch diameter hail and a 67 mph wind gust was reported from this system. Multiple reports of 2 inches of rain were also reported with the core of this system which caused flooding to occur in Wallace county for the following days. Another round of heavy rain occurred on the 17th, which worsened the flooding that began on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters were reported to be flowing over multiple roads along the Smoky River. This continued for days after the rain ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex of thunderstorms brings large hail and damaging wind gusts at rather odd time of day to portions of north central Kansas on May 9th. The severe thunderstorms that brought large hail up to half dollar size, and wind gusts around 60 MPH, actually developed over the high plains the previous evening and overnight. This activity arrived into NW portions of Phillips County shortly after 0900CDT, which is an unusual time for severe weather in this portion of the country. The MCS evolved into a mix of bowing segments and embedded supercells as they crossed north central Kansas and resulted in a mix of significant severe wind gusts and large hail. The strongest wind gusts ranged from 70 to 80 MPH, and the largest reported hail was up to tennis ball size. There is a climatological minimum in severe weather during 0600-1200 local time frame in this part of the country, so it is even more unusual that there were instances of both significant severe hail and wind. The severe portion of this MCS exited north central Kansas by around 1300CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by the Smith Center Airport AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Middle to upper level winds were out of the west to southwest, and an upper level disturbance moved over the region from Colorado by the early evening hours. There was a weak surface low to the south with an inverted trough axis over portions of north central Kansas and south central Nebraska extending north of the low. This trough axis set up a frontal zone across Nebraska and Kansas. High based thunderstorms developed to the west of the area along a shortwave trough then moved eastward and encountered increased instability. Mixed layer CAPE was around 1000 to 2000 J/kg and deep layer wind shear was around 50 to 60 knots. There was a slight risk of severe storms from the Storm Prediction Center. ||Storms began developing to the west and northwest of the area during the afternoon hours. A cluster of storms moved in from the west and began impacting north central Kansas around 7 pm. This cluster of storms continued moving eastward during the evening hours and extended northward into portions of southern Nebraska. The cluster exited the area around 10:30 pm as it continued tracking eastward across Nebraska. There were several strong to severe wind gusts across north central Kansas and one report of golfball sized hail. One report of thunderstorm wind damage occurred in Mitchell County. The storms went on to produce a few severe wind gusts in Nuckolls and Thayer Counties in Nebraska as well as a wind damage report in Thayer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 68 MPH was measured by a trained spotter located three miles east-northeast of Woodston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex of thunderstorms brings large hail and damaging wind gusts at rather odd time of day to portions of north central Kansas on May 9th. The severe thunderstorms that brought large hail up to half dollar size, and wind gusts around 60 MPH, actually developed over the high plains the previous evening and overnight. This activity arrived into NW portions of Phillips County shortly after 0900CDT, which is an unusual time for severe weather in this portion of the country. The MCS evolved into a mix of bowing segments and embedded supercells as they crossed north central Kansas and resulted in a mix of significant severe wind gusts and large hail. The strongest wind gusts ranged from 70 to 80 MPH, and the largest reported hail was up to tennis ball size. There is a climatological minimum in severe weather during 0600-1200 local time frame in this part of the country, so it is even more unusual that there were instances of both significant severe hail and wind. The severe portion of this MCS exited north central Kansas by around 1300CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Middle to upper level winds were out of the west to southwest, and an upper level disturbance moved over the region from Colorado by the early evening hours. There was a weak surface low to the south with an inverted trough axis over portions of north central Kansas and south central Nebraska extending north of the low. This trough axis set up a frontal zone across Nebraska and Kansas. High based thunderstorms developed to the west of the area along a shortwave trough then moved eastward and encountered increased instability. Mixed layer CAPE was around 1000 to 2000 J/kg and deep layer wind shear was around 50 to 60 knots. There was a slight risk of severe storms from the Storm Prediction Center. ||Storms began developing to the west and northwest of the area during the afternoon hours. A cluster of storms moved in from the west and began impacting north central Kansas around 7 pm. This cluster of storms continued moving eastward during the evening hours and extended northward into portions of southern Nebraska. The cluster exited the area around 10:30 pm as it continued tracking eastward across Nebraska. There were several strong to severe wind gusts across north central Kansas and one report of golfball sized hail. One report of thunderstorm wind damage occurred in Mitchell County. The storms went on to produce a few severe wind gusts in Nuckolls and Thayer Counties in Nebraska as well as a wind damage report in Thayer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 71 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located one mile south-southwest of Stockton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this North Central Kansas area was spared a much nastier combination of wind-driven hail that walloped parts of far southern Nebraska just off to the north-northwest, it was still impacted by a pair of severe storms during the predawn hours on this Thursday...including a very long-lived supercell. All ground-truth storm reports were associated with this supercell/compact cluster as it charged across Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties between 3:45-6:00 a.m. CDT, with a sampling of reports including: measured gusts of 62 MPH near Lovewell Reservoir (unofficial mesonet) and 58 MPH at Phillipsburg airport (AWOS); quarter size hail near Long Island. Although there were no reports of significant/widespread damage, a small garage was destroyed east of Mankato and some tree damage was confirmed in Smith Center and Jewell. To the southwest of the primary supercell, a smaller isolated severe storm reached radar-indicated severe levels as it traversed the Osborne-Smith County line, but there was no ground-truth verification. ||Breaking down event evolution/timing, in the mid-upper levels, although lacking any noteworthy, small-scale disturbances, broad and seasonably-strong west-southwesterly flow was in place over the Central Plains...downstream from a large scale, positively-tilted trough centered from the Northern Rockies to the Great Basin. At the surface, North Central Kansas resided slightly north of a quasi-stationary front stretched across the state from southwest-to-northeast, with resultant east-northeasterly breezes to the north of this boundary enhancing High Plains upslope flow. Turning to storm-scale details, the primary supercell that eventually slipped into the local area actually initiated/became severe roughly 280 miles off to the west-northwest during the late afternoon of the 28th...in extreme southeast Wyoming. During the evening, the intense storm slowly lumbered southeastward through the far southwestern Nebraska Panhandle into northeast Colorado. By around midnight CDT, this convection had morphed into a powerful supercell cluster/small complex as it entered far southwestern Nebraska and started tracking more due-east...riding along the northern fringes of a tight mid-level temperature gradient (generally hugging the 12C line at 700 millibars). After rolling across southwestern into far south central Nebraska for several hours, the far southern (and slightly less vigorous) fringes of the supercell cluster reached northwestern Phillips County around 3:45 a.m. CDT. Over the next few hours, it tracked east-southeast along/near the Highway 36 corridor...before exiting the local area out of eastern Jewell County by 6 a.m. CDT. Back to the southwest and in the wake of the passage of the dominant storm complex, the aforementioned briefly-severe-warned storm tracked along the Osborne-Smith County line between 5:45-6:30 a.m. CDT before weakening, with North Central Kansas almost entirely convection-free by 7 a.m. CDT. The mesoscale environment strongly supported a long-lived, persistent supercell cluster, featuring a formidable combination of at least 2000-3000 most-unstable CAPE and  50-60 knots of deep-layer wind shear.  That being said, from a forecast perspective, this was not an overly-obvious setup for an early morning severe threat. Although some necessary ingredients were clearly present, short term/higher-resolution computer models (CAMs) varied considerably regarding how evening High Plains convection would evolve, and to what degree it would impact locales farther east (including North Central Kansas). The 01 UTC (8 p.m. CDT) Day 1 Convective Outlook from SPC (valid through the early morning of the 29th) highlighted only a Marginal Risk (level 1 of 5) for North Central Kansas, but also acknowledged very substantial uncertainty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall amounts occurred across Northwest Kansas during the month. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early afternoon of the 30th, storms from Colorado crossed into Kansas along I-70. These storms caused widespread wind damage and up to 2 inch in diameter hail. The highest measured wind gust was 79.4 mph at Hill City and then the station promptly stopped reporting winds. One injury did occur when a vehicle was blown off of the road. Torrential rainfall also lead to localized flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought conditions expanded across portions southeastern Kansas through the end of June. Little to no rainfall allowed drought conditions to deteriorate over most of southeastern Kansas during the month. Across southeastern Kansas Severe (D2) drought expanded or persisted with Moderate (D1) Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limited periods of rainfall through the month proved little benefit for much of the county allowing Severe (D2) Drought to expand across the entire county, drought conditions remained across the region through the month of June. The US Drought Monitor indicated all of the county as being in Severe (D2) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex of thunderstorms brings large hail and damaging wind gusts at rather odd time of day to portions of north central Kansas on May 9th. The severe thunderstorms that brought large hail up to half dollar size, and wind gusts around 60 MPH, actually developed over the high plains the previous evening and overnight. This activity arrived into NW portions of Phillips County shortly after 0900CDT, which is an unusual time for severe weather in this portion of the country. The MCS evolved into a mix of bowing segments and embedded supercells as they crossed north central Kansas and resulted in a mix of significant severe wind gusts and large hail. The strongest wind gusts ranged from 70 to 80 MPH, and the largest reported hail was up to tennis ball size. There is a climatological minimum in severe weather during 0600-1200 local time frame in this part of the country, so it is even more unusual that there were instances of both significant severe hail and wind. The severe portion of this MCS exited north central Kansas by around 1300CDT.

Above normal rainfall amounts occurred across Northwest Kansas during the month.  This allowed for a slight reduction in the counties affected by the severe and exceptional drought, namely Thomas, Greeley, and Wichita counties.  Rainfall amounts in the counties still affected by severe or worse drought ranged from 3-11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts for the month ranged from three to eight inches, with the highest rainfall amounts in the eastern part of the county.  This was near normal to four inches above the normal amount of three to four inches of rainfall for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early afternoon of the 16th, a system of thunderstorms formed in east central Colorado and progressed eastward into Kansas. The severe cells of this system remained about 30 miles south of I-70 until it was near the Oakley area, where it began a northeastward trend. Up to 2 inch diameter hail and a 67 mph wind gust was reported from this system. Multiple reports of 2 inches of rain were also reported with the core of this system which caused flooding to occur in Wallace county for the following days. Another round of heavy rain occurred on the 17th, which worsened the flooding that began on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least quarter size hail in Grinnell. Tree leaves shredded. Hail lasted for 10-15 minutes and covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A linear cluster of storms developed off the Front Range moving east across Northwest Kansas during the evening hours. The storms produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 100 mph as they moved through, with the strongest gusts reported in the town of McDonald in Rawlins County. Damage occurred primarily to trees in portions of Cheyenne and Rawlins counties with additional damage to powerlines, campers, and structures. The thunderstorm wind gusts and the outflow of the storm also created dust storm conditions with visibilities of one-quarter mile or less observed in Cheyenne, Sherman, and Rawlins counties. Blowing dust with visibility reductions of three to five miles were also observed near the cities of Colby and Oakley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm with estimated 90 mph straight line winds gusts flipped and destroyed a 5th wheel camper and a bumper pull camper. An outbuilding and tree were also damaged. All debris was reported to be scattered to the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During June 2023, the vast majority of this six-county North Central Kansas area spent the entire month assigned at least Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM) as a long-term drought situation persisted (only a small part of southeast Mitchell County featured slightly-better-off Moderate Drought/D1 throughout the month). In fact, June marked the 17th-consecutive month with at-least-D2 plaguing portions of the local area, and the third-straight month with at least limited coverage of worst-off Extreme Drought (D3) and Exceptional Drought (D4). However, after North Central Kansas saw its drought situation stoop to its overall-worst level in a decade during May, June featured some much-needed positive trends. Although it was not an overly-wet month, widespread near-to-modestly-above normal rainfall actually brought 1-category improvement to roughly half of the area, with only limited locations experiencing 1-category degradation. Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics during June, the month opened with roughly 95 percent of the six-county area assigned D2-or-worse (only a limited stripe of D1 across eastern Mitchell and extreme southeast Jewell counties). Of greater concern, roughly two-thirds of the domain featured D3-or worse and around one-third of the area contained worst-off D4 (including the vast majority of Rooks/Osborne counties). As June kicked into gear, drought improvement got underway with the first USDM issuance of the month, as a decent chunk of the area saw a 1-category change for the better...including noticeable reductions in D4 within mainly Rooks/Osborne counties and decreases in D3 mainly within Phillips/Smith counties. Then, a few weeks later, additional 1-category improvement took place...highlighted by a complete removal of D4 from Osborne and extreme western Mitchell counties, and removal of D2 from northwestern Phillips County. Unfortunately, the final June USDM issuance did bring some 1-category degradation to eastern parts of the area, although this mainly involved much of Jewell and northeastern Mitchell counties simply reverting back to D2 (which is where things stood for most of this area when the month began). At month's end, the drought situation stood as follows: 1) Worst-off D4 lingered within only western Rooks County...2) D3 encompassed most of primarily Osborne, Rooks and western Mitchell counties...3) D2 prevailed across most of the remainder of the area...4) Small zones of locally-best-off D1 resided in northwestern Phillips and southeastern Mitchell counties. ||Turning to June 2023 precipitation details, overall it was good news (especially compared to recent dry months). The majority of North Central Kansas registered between 2-6 inches...generally 60-170 percent of normal. However, the vast majority of the area received at least two-thirds of normal rainfall, meaning that even the drier spots were not acutely dry. Per around 30 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, some of the wettest June tallies featured: 6.22 in Lebanon, 6.03 in Formoso, 5.82 at Smith Center and 5.75 at Lovewell Dam. On the opposite end of the measuring stick, among the lowest monthly totals were: 1.72 near Damar, 1.95 in Logan, 2.16 ten miles west-northwest of Stockton and 2.36 in Plainville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions improved across portions southeastern Kansas through the end of May. Beneficial rainfall allowed drought conditions to improve over most of southeastern Kansas during the month. Across southeastern Kansas Extreme (D3) drought was removed  thanks to several rounds of rainfall. Severe (D2) Drought persisted however with Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) around the Severe Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month proved beneficial for much of the county improving from Extreme (D3) Drought, though drought conditions remained across the region through the month of May. The US Drought Monitor indicated the northern half of the county as remaining in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Early spring crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this North Central Kansas area was spared a much nastier combination of wind-driven hail that walloped parts of far southern Nebraska just off to the north-northwest, it was still impacted by a pair of severe storms during the predawn hours on this Thursday...including a very long-lived supercell. All ground-truth storm reports were associated with this supercell/compact cluster as it charged across Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties between 3:45-6:00 a.m. CDT, with a sampling of reports including: measured gusts of 62 MPH near Lovewell Reservoir (unofficial mesonet) and 58 MPH at Phillipsburg airport (AWOS); quarter size hail near Long Island. Although there were no reports of significant/widespread damage, a small garage was destroyed east of Mankato and some tree damage was confirmed in Smith Center and Jewell. To the southwest of the primary supercell, a smaller isolated severe storm reached radar-indicated severe levels as it traversed the Osborne-Smith County line, but there was no ground-truth verification. ||Breaking down event evolution/timing, in the mid-upper levels, although lacking any noteworthy, small-scale disturbances, broad and seasonably-strong west-southwesterly flow was in place over the Central Plains...downstream from a large scale, positively-tilted trough centered from the Northern Rockies to the Great Basin. At the surface, North Central Kansas resided slightly north of a quasi-stationary front stretched across the state from southwest-to-northeast, with resultant east-northeasterly breezes to the north of this boundary enhancing High Plains upslope flow. Turning to storm-scale details, the primary supercell that eventually slipped into the local area actually initiated/became severe roughly 280 miles off to the west-northwest during the late afternoon of the 28th...in extreme southeast Wyoming. During the evening, the intense storm slowly lumbered southeastward through the far southwestern Nebraska Panhandle into northeast Colorado. By around midnight CDT, this convection had morphed into a powerful supercell cluster/small complex as it entered far southwestern Nebraska and started tracking more due-east...riding along the northern fringes of a tight mid-level temperature gradient (generally hugging the 12C line at 700 millibars). After rolling across southwestern into far south central Nebraska for several hours, the far southern (and slightly less vigorous) fringes of the supercell cluster reached northwestern Phillips County around 3:45 a.m. CDT. Over the next few hours, it tracked east-southeast along/near the Highway 36 corridor...before exiting the local area out of eastern Jewell County by 6 a.m. CDT. Back to the southwest and in the wake of the passage of the dominant storm complex, the aforementioned briefly-severe-warned storm tracked along the Osborne-Smith County line between 5:45-6:30 a.m. CDT before weakening, with North Central Kansas almost entirely convection-free by 7 a.m. CDT. The mesoscale environment strongly supported a long-lived, persistent supercell cluster, featuring a formidable combination of at least 2000-3000 most-unstable CAPE and  50-60 knots of deep-layer wind shear.  That being said, from a forecast perspective, this was not an overly-obvious setup for an early morning severe threat. Although some necessary ingredients were clearly present, short term/higher-resolution computer models (CAMs) varied considerably regarding how evening High Plains convection would evolve, and to what degree it would impact locales farther east (including North Central Kansas). The 01 UTC (8 p.m. CDT) Day 1 Convective Outlook from SPC (valid through the early morning of the 29th) highlighted only a Marginal Risk (level 1 of 5) for North Central Kansas, but also acknowledged very substantial uncertainty.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by the Phillipsburg Airport AWOS.

Back to the southwest and in the wake of the passage of the dominant storm complex, the aforementioned briefly-severe-warned storm tracked along the Osborne-Smith County line between 5:45-6:30 a.m. CDT before weakening, with North Central Kansas almost entirely convection-free by 7 a.m. CDT. The mesoscale environment strongly supported a long-lived, persistent supercell cluster, featuring a formidable combination of at least 2000-3000 most-unstable CAPE and  50-60 knots of deep-layer wind shear.  That being said, from a forecast perspective, this was not an overly-obvious setup for an early morning severe threat. Although some necessary ingredients were clearly present, short term/higher-resolution computer models (CAMs) varied considerably regarding how evening High Plains convection would evolve, and to what degree it would impact locales farther east (including North Central Kansas). The 01 UTC (8 p.m. CDT) Day 1 Convective Outlook from SPC (valid through the early morning of the 29th) highlighted only a Marginal Risk (level 1 of 5) for North Central Kansas, but also acknowledged very substantial uncertainty.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 62 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located near Lovewell Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions improved across portions southeastern Kansas through the end of May. Beneficial rainfall allowed drought conditions to improve over most of southeastern Kansas during the month. Across southeastern Kansas Extreme (D3) drought was removed  thanks to several rounds of rainfall. Severe (D2) Drought persisted however with Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) around the Severe Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month proved beneficial for much of the county improving from Extreme (D3) Drought, though drought conditions remained across the region through the month of May. The US Drought Monitor indicated that most of the county remained in Moderate (D1) Drought with the southeastern part of the county in Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions to end the month. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Early spring crop and vegetation growth showed signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop loss estimates from the beginning of January 2023 are included in this report though changes may occur when the drought ends and final loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During June 2023, the vast majority of this six-county North Central Kansas area spent the entire month assigned at least Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM) as a long-term drought situation persisted (only a small part of southeast Mitchell County featured slightly-better-off Moderate Drought/D1 throughout the month). In fact, June marked the 17th-consecutive month with at-least-D2 plaguing portions of the local area, and the third-straight month with at least limited coverage of worst-off Extreme Drought (D3) and Exceptional Drought (D4). However, after North Central Kansas saw its drought situation stoop to its overall-worst level in a decade during May, June featured some much-needed positive trends. Although it was not an overly-wet month, widespread near-to-modestly-above normal rainfall actually brought 1-category improvement to roughly half of the area, with only limited locations experiencing 1-category degradation. Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics during June, the month opened with roughly 95 percent of the six-county area assigned D2-or-worse (only a limited stripe of D1 across eastern Mitchell and extreme southeast Jewell counties). Of greater concern, roughly two-thirds of the domain featured D3-or worse and around one-third of the area contained worst-off D4 (including the vast majority of Rooks/Osborne counties). As June kicked into gear, drought improvement got underway with the first USDM issuance of the month, as a decent chunk of the area saw a 1-category change for the better...including noticeable reductions in D4 within mainly Rooks/Osborne counties and decreases in D3 mainly within Phillips/Smith counties. Then, a few weeks later, additional 1-category improvement took place...highlighted by a complete removal of D4 from Osborne and extreme western Mitchell counties, and removal of D2 from northwestern Phillips County. Unfortunately, the final June USDM issuance did bring some 1-category degradation to eastern parts of the area, although this mainly involved much of Jewell and northeastern Mitchell counties simply reverting back to D2 (which is where things stood for most of this area when the month began). At month's end, the drought situation stood as follows: 1) Worst-off D4 lingered within only western Rooks County...2) D3 encompassed most of primarily Osborne, Rooks and western Mitchell counties...3) D2 prevailed across most of the remainder of the area...4) Small zones of locally-best-off D1 resided in northwestern Phillips and southeastern Mitchell counties. ||Turning to June 2023 precipitation details, overall it was good news (especially compared to recent dry months). The majority of North Central Kansas registered between 2-6 inches...generally 60-170 percent of normal. However, the vast majority of the area received at least two-thirds of normal rainfall, meaning that even the drier spots were not acutely dry. Per around 30 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, some of the wettest June tallies featured: 6.22 in Lebanon, 6.03 in Formoso, 5.82 at Smith Center and 5.75 at Lovewell Dam. On the opposite end of the measuring stick, among the lowest monthly totals were: 1.72 near Damar, 1.95 in Logan, 2.16 ten miles west-northwest of Stockton and 2.36 in Plainville.

A broad upper level trough was over the western part of the country and another upper trough was over the Great Lakes and extended south across much of the eastern part of the country. Northwest upper level winds were over north central Kansas. A shortwave trough moved across the area during the evening hours. Mid-level lapse rates were generally 7 to 8 degrees C/km across north central Kansas. MUCAPE was between 3,000 to 4,000 J/km and effective bulk shear was between 40 and 50 knots. ||Storms developed across northern Kansas and southern Nebraska during the afternoon hours. Some storms in southern Nebraska moved south into north central Kansas. The storms began by producing nickel size hail at 3:10 PM CDT in Smith County. The storms also impacted Phillips and Osborne Counties. Golf ball sized hail in Smith County resulted in damage to vehicles and homes. The largest hail reported was three inches in Osborne County at 4:16 PM CDT. The storms ended by producing a 65 mph wind gust in Osborne County at 4:19 PM CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was accompanied by wind gusts estimated to be near 55 MPH.

A linear cluster of storms developed off the Front Range moving east across Northwest Kansas during the evening hours. The storms produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 100 mph as they moved through, with the strongest gusts reported in the town of McDonald in Rawlins County. Damage occurred primarily to trees in portions of Cheyenne and Rawlins counties with additional damage to powerlines, campers, and structures. The thunderstorm wind gusts and the outflow of the storm also created dust storm conditions with visibilities of one-quarter mile or less observed in Cheyenne, Sherman, and Rawlins counties. Blowing dust with visibility reductions of three to five miles were also observed near the cities of Colby and Oakley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was centered over Canada with a portion of the upper trough extending south to the Gulf Coast. Temperatures during the day had warmed up into the 80s and 90s. Relatively high CAPE values were across the area with 0 to 6 kilometer wind shear values generally between 40 and 50 knots. Mid-level lapse rates were generally between 7 to 8 degrees Celsius/kilometer. A weak cold front was pushing south through Nebraska into Kansas during the evening which provided an additional source of lift.||A line of storms was moving south from south central Nebraska and intersected with a line of storms moving eastward across northern Kansas. These two lines of storms merged and slowly moved eastward during the evening hours across north central Kansas. These storms produced severe wind gusts across Phillips and Smith Counties. The first and strongest wind report was 63 mph at 9:54 PM CDT near Phillipsburg, Kansas which produced some damage. The last report was 60 mph at 11 PM CDT which resulted in tree damage in Lebanon, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 63 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located two miles west-southwest of Phillipsburg. In the Agra area, power pole damage was reported, and a roof was peeled back from an old abandoned building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough was centered over Canada and extended southward over the Central Plains to the East Coast. Upper level disturbances had moved over the Central Plains at the base of the trough and provided lift. One of the upper disturbances moved over north central Kansas in the late morning to early afternoon hours. CAPE was fairly high across the area and zero to six km wind shear was generally between 30 to 45 knots. Mid-level lapse rates were generally between 7 to 8 degrees C/km.||Storms developed across northern Kansas during the late morning hours of July 14th. Storms were also moving southward into Kansas from Nebraska. A storm had moved southward from Nebraska into Phillips County in Kansas and produced golf ball size hail at 11:45 AM CDT. This storm also produced a wind gust of 75 mph in Phillips County that resulted in tree damage and continued southeastward to produce nickel size hail in Rooks County. Finally, the storm produced wind gusts estimated near 60 mph in Osborne County at 12:40 PM CDT that resulted in tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over most of the southern half of the country. A weak upper level disturbance was moving across northern Nebraska. A weak surface low pressure was across far north central Kansas during the afternoon hours. Effective bulk shear values were generally around 30 knots, and most unstable CAPE was generally 3,000 to 5,000 J/kg.  Temperatures were in the triple digits, and dewpoints were in the mid to upper 60s. ||Storms developed and moved across north central Kansas during the late afternoon and early evening hours of July 28th. A storm developed over Jewell County and produced two inch hail after 5:30 PM CDT. Another storm strengthened over Mitchell County and produced one inch hail which resulted in crop damage at 6 PM CDT. This storm weakened and another storm produced one inch hail which also resulted in crop damage in the same county after 6:30 PM CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind-driven hail resulted in widespread damage to corn, soybean, and milo crops. Full grown milo cut down to three inches. Estimated three mile wide swath centered roughly around 350 Road and Q Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge extended from the Desert Southwest and south Texas northward to Nebraska and Wyoming. Temperatures were mostly in the 90s during the early to mid-evening hours across north central Kansas. Dewpoints were in the upper 60s to lower 70s. There was a temperature gradient across northern Kansas with temperatures in the 70s and 80s across northeast Kansas and temperatures around 100 degrees in central Kansas. Effective wind shear was generally 30 to 40 knots across north central Kansas and most unstable CAPE was around 3,000 J/kg. Mid-level lapse rates were between 7 and 8 degrees C/km. Storms developed across north central Kansas during the afternoon hours. One storm developed and strengthened in Mitchell County and produced wind gusts estimated to be near 65 mph. This resulted in some minor tree damage. This storm then weakened and exited the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 65 MPH resulted in some minor tree damage eight miles east of Victor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed across portions of western Nebraska early of the morning of July 14th. Storms pushed through much of the eastern 2/3rds of Kansas causing widespread wind damage with recorded wind gusts up to 85 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a measured wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 14, 2023 a strong storm formed in northeast Kansas and eventually transformed into a strong squall line that impacted far eastern Kansas and western Missouri. This storm left a swath of wind damage mainly through the heart of the Kansas City metro area in the form of widespread tree damage. Several structures were impacted by falling trees as the storm moved through the city, and into central Missouri. The highest reported wind gust came from western Johnson County where a personal weather station recorded 89 mph wind just west of Lenexa. Numerous wind reports, both estimated and measured were over 70 mph. At one point nearly 200,000 people across the Kansas City metro lost power. As of the next day, roughly 60,000 Kansas Citians were still without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 60 mph winds just west of De Soto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough was centered over Canada and extended southward over the Central Plains to the East Coast. Upper level disturbances had moved over the Central Plains at the base of the trough and provided lift. One of the upper disturbances moved over north central Kansas in the late morning to early afternoon hours. CAPE was fairly high across the area and zero to six km wind shear was generally between 30 to 45 knots. Mid-level lapse rates were generally between 7 to 8 degrees C/km.||Storms developed across northern Kansas during the late morning hours of July 14th. Storms were also moving southward into Kansas from Nebraska. A storm had moved southward from Nebraska into Phillips County in Kansas and produced golf ball size hail at 11:45 AM CDT. This storm also produced a wind gust of 75 mph in Phillips County that resulted in tree damage and continued southeastward to produce nickel size hail in Rooks County. Finally, the storm produced wind gusts estimated near 60 mph in Osborne County at 12:40 PM CDT that resulted in tree damage.

A linear cluster of storms developed off the Front Range moving east across Northwest Kansas during the evening hours. The storms produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 100 mph as they moved through, with the strongest gusts reported in the town of McDonald in Rawlins County. Damage occurred primarily to trees in portions of Cheyenne and Rawlins counties with additional damage to powerlines, campers, and structures. The thunderstorm wind gusts and the outflow of the storm also created dust storm conditions with visibilities of one-quarter mile or less observed in Cheyenne, Sherman, and Rawlins counties. Blowing dust with visibility reductions of three to five miles were also observed near the cities of Colby and Oakley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of 50 yards was reported due to a thunderstorm-caused dust storm. Winds increased between the outflow and the storms with a nearby mesonet station observing a gust of 56 mph at 19:35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While some locations saw improvement from Extreme (D3) Drought by the end of July Extreme drought remained for portions of Bourbon and Crawford Counties. Severe (D2) Drought conditions expanded across portions southeastern Kansas through the end of July. What rainfall occurred during the month provided little improvement for drought conditions with some areas deteriorating over southeastern Kansas during the month. Across southeastern Kansas Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) drought expanded or persisted with Moderate (D1) Drought areas making up the remainder of the area. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limited periods of rainfall through the month allowed Extreme (D3) Drought to slightly decrease over parts of Bourbon County in July. Impacts across the region continue over larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex of thunderstorms brings large hail and damaging wind gusts at rather odd time of day to portions of north central Kansas on May 9th. The severe thunderstorms that brought large hail up to half dollar size, and wind gusts around 60 MPH, actually developed over the high plains the previous evening and overnight. This activity arrived into NW portions of Phillips County shortly after 0900CDT, which is an unusual time for severe weather in this portion of the country. The MCS evolved into a mix of bowing segments and embedded supercells as they crossed north central Kansas and resulted in a mix of significant severe wind gusts and large hail. The strongest wind gusts ranged from 70 to 80 MPH, and the largest reported hail was up to tennis ball size. There is a climatological minimum in severe weather during 0600-1200 local time frame in this part of the country, so it is even more unusual that there were instances of both significant severe hail and wind. The severe portion of this MCS exited north central Kansas by around 1300CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Middle to upper level winds were out of the west to southwest, and an upper level disturbance moved over the region from Colorado by the early evening hours. There was a weak surface low to the south with an inverted trough axis over portions of north central Kansas and south central Nebraska extending north of the low. This trough axis set up a frontal zone across Nebraska and Kansas. High based thunderstorms developed to the west of the area along a shortwave trough then moved eastward and encountered increased instability. Mixed layer CAPE was around 1000 to 2000 J/kg and deep layer wind shear was around 50 to 60 knots. There was a slight risk of severe storms from the Storm Prediction Center. ||Storms began developing to the west and northwest of the area during the afternoon hours. A cluster of storms moved in from the west and began impacting north central Kansas around 7 pm. This cluster of storms continued moving eastward during the evening hours and extended northward into portions of southern Nebraska. The cluster exited the area around 10:30 pm as it continued tracking eastward across Nebraska. There were several strong to severe wind gusts across north central Kansas and one report of golfball sized hail. One report of thunderstorm wind damage occurred in Mitchell County. The storms went on to produce a few severe wind gusts in Nuckolls and Thayer Counties in Nebraska as well as a wind damage report in Thayer County.
Three counties in far eastern Kansas were within severe (D2) drought for August 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) persisted through all of August 2023 in Miami County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought was present in Johnson County, KS through mid August, after which well targeted rainfall improved drought conditions to D1 or better.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting on August 19, 2023 well-above normal temperatures impacted the central part of the United States, kicking off a heatwave that has not been experienced in the region in recent history. Kansas City International Airport reached high temperatures not recorded in the area since August of 2012. In contrast to the 2012 heatwave, this stretch of hot weather was accompanied by extremely high dewpoints, with frequently reached the upper 70s to middle 80s across the entire area. The combination of the extreme heat and the excessively high dewpoints led to heat indices from August 19 through August 25 to rise to 120-130 degrees. The hottest heat index in the area occurred in Lawrence, Kansas on the 20th and 21st, when it rose to 134 degrees.||Perhaps of note, the Topeka upper air program, which has sent radiosondes into the atmosphere twice per day since 1955 had only measured 6000 meters to the 500 millibar level two times in its history. During this heatwave Topeka measured 6000 meters to 500 mb on six straight launches, spanning three days. The highest 500 mb level measured out of Topeka for this stretch was 6030 meters, a testament to the extreme nature of this mid level ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme heat and humidity leading to excessively hot heat indices impacted Johnson County KS from August 19th through the 25th. ||The daily max heat index at either Johnson County Executive (OJC) and New Century Air Center (IXD) are as follows:||August 19: 115|August 20: 122|August 21: 118|August 22: 118|August 23: 110|August 24: 109|August 25: 108.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex of thunderstorms brings large hail and damaging wind gusts at rather odd time of day to portions of north central Kansas on May 9th. The severe thunderstorms that brought large hail up to half dollar size, and wind gusts around 60 MPH, actually developed over the high plains the previous evening and overnight. This activity arrived into NW portions of Phillips County shortly after 0900CDT, which is an unusual time for severe weather in this portion of the country. The MCS evolved into a mix of bowing segments and embedded supercells as they crossed north central Kansas and resulted in a mix of significant severe wind gusts and large hail. The strongest wind gusts ranged from 70 to 80 MPH, and the largest reported hail was up to tennis ball size. There is a climatological minimum in severe weather during 0600-1200 local time frame in this part of the country, so it is even more unusual that there were instances of both significant severe hail and wind. The severe portion of this MCS exited north central Kansas by around 1300CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of tennis balls was reported in Mankato.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One tornado was documented that moved north out of Chase county into far southeast Morris County around 905 pm CDT.   The tornado did damage to trees and one home before ending around 3 miles south southwest of Dunlap, Kansas.  Other storms produced large hail and damaging winds.  A second line of severe storms developed after midnight during the early morning hours of April 20th and produced wind damage most concentrated in Clay County in the city of Clay Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Results of an NWS damage survey revealed tree damage and damage to a home approximately 3 miles SSW of Dunlap, Kansas. The home had portions of the roof removed and windows broken. This appeared to be the end of the tornado track that moved north out of Chase County.  The tornado appears to have crossed from Chase into Morris County around 905pm and ended around 908 pm CDT based on radar data.  Based on DOD to DI winds estimated to be around 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex of thunderstorms brings large hail and damaging wind gusts at rather odd time of day to portions of north central Kansas on May 9th. The severe thunderstorms that brought large hail up to half dollar size, and wind gusts around 60 MPH, actually developed over the high plains the previous evening and overnight. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 81 MPH was measured by a Kansas State Mesonet site located one mile north-northwest of Osborne. Received a report from television station KAKE of damage near this mesonet location, and confirmed with county dispatch that at least three power poles were damaged or destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this North Central Kansas area was spared a much nastier combination of wind-driven hail that walloped parts of far southern Nebraska just off to the north-northwest, it was still impacted by a pair of severe storms during the predawn hours on this Thursday...including a very long-lived supercell. All ground-truth storm reports were associated with this supercell/compact cluster as it charged across Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties between 3:45-6:00 a.m. CDT, with a sampling of reports including: measured gusts of 62 MPH near Lovewell Reservoir (unofficial mesonet) and 58 MPH at Phillipsburg airport (AWOS); quarter size hail near Long Island. Although there were no reports of significant/widespread damage, a small garage was destroyed east of Mankato and some tree damage was confirmed in Smith Center and Jewell. To the southwest of the primary supercell, a smaller isolated severe storm reached radar-indicated severe levels as it traversed the Osborne-Smith County line, but there was no ground-truth verification. ||Breaking down event evolution/timing, in the mid-upper levels, although lacking any noteworthy, small-scale disturbances, broad and seasonably-strong west-southwesterly flow was in place over the Central Plains...downstream from a large scale, positively-tilted trough centered from the Northern Rockies to the Great Basin. At the surface, North Central Kansas resided slightly north of a quasi-stationary front stretched across the state from southwest-to-northeast, with resultant east-northeasterly breezes to the north of this boundary enhancing High Plains upslope flow. Turning to storm-scale details, the primary supercell that eventually slipped into the local area actually initiated/became severe roughly 280 miles off to the west-northwest during the late afternoon of the 28th...in extreme southeast Wyoming. During the evening, the intense storm slowly lumbered southeastward through the far southwestern Nebraska Panhandle into northeast Colorado. By around midnight CDT, this convection had morphed into a powerful supercell cluster/small complex as it entered far southwestern Nebraska and started tracking more due-east...riding along the northern fringes of a tight mid-level temperature gradient (generally hugging the 12C line at 700 millibars). After rolling across southwestern into far south central Nebraska for several hours, the far southern (and slightly less vigorous) fringes of the supercell cluster reached northwestern Phillips County around 3:45 a.m. CDT. Over the next few hours, it tracked east-southeast along/near the Highway 36 corridor...before exiting the local area out of eastern Jewell County by 6 a.m. CDT. Back to the southwest and in the wake of the passage of the dominant storm complex, the aforementioned briefly-severe-warned storm tracked along the Osborne-Smith County line between 5:45-6:30 a.m. CDT before weakening, with North Central Kansas almost entirely convection-free by 7 a.m. CDT. The mesoscale environment strongly supported a long-lived, persistent supercell cluster, featuring a formidable combination of at least 2000-3000 most-unstable CAPE and  50-60 knots of deep-layer wind shear.  That being said, from a forecast perspective, this was not an overly-obvious setup for an early morning severe threat. Although some necessary ingredients were clearly present, short term/higher-resolution computer models (CAMs) varied considerably regarding how evening High Plains convection would evolve, and to what degree it would impact locales farther east (including North Central Kansas). The 01 UTC (8 p.m. CDT) Day 1 Convective Outlook from SPC (valid through the early morning of the 29th) highlighted only a Marginal Risk (level 1 of 5) for North Central Kansas, but also acknowledged very substantial uncertainty.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH caused minor tree damage in Smith Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 7-10 p.m. CDT on this Friday evening, a compact mesoscale convective system (MCS) with an initially intense, embedded supercell rolled from west-to-east across primarily Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties in this North Central Kansas area, producing several reports of damaging winds/large hail (including up to tennis ball size). This severe storm cluster first organized in northeast Colorado during the early-mid afternoon before traversing northwest Kansas mid-late afternoon and eventually infiltrating mainly the northern half of Rooks County between 7-8 p.m. CDT, during which time the dominant supercell reached its peak intensity within the local area. In Rooks County, there were several reports of hail ranging from ping pong ball up to tennis ball size, and broken windows were reported at a business south of Stockton along Highway 183. Also in Rooks County, damaging winds estimated up to 70 MPH caused tree damage in Damar and overturned a camper at Webster State Park. Between 8-9 p.m. CDT, the dominant supercell weakened slightly over Osborne County as it merged with other convection drifting north out of central Kansas, but it still yielded a report of ping pong ball size hail eight miles south-southeast of Osborne. Finally, between 9-10 p.m. CDT the most intense convection transitioned to a more linear mode as it crossed Mitchell County, yielding an unofficial mesonet-measured wind gust of 59 MPH seven miles east of Tipton. Although the main storm cluster departed east of the local area by 10 p.m. CDT, trailing showers/weaker storms lingered for at least a few hours before convection departed North Central Kansas for the night. Rainfall-wise, although limited parts of the area received at least 1-2 inches in a short time, there were no reports of significant flooding. ||Turning to the meteorological background, this was a fairly classic setup for early-summer MCS development. In the mid-upper levels, the primary driver was a shortwave trough slowly migrating from the CO/WY/NE border area into the Central Plains over the course of the afternoon-evening. At the surface, North Central Kansas resided slightly north of a generally west-east oriented quasi-stationary front stretched from southern into east central Kansas. Locally, the early evening pre-convective environment was defined by temperatures in the 80s and dewpoints in the 60s (F), with mesoscale parameters featuring moderate mixed-layer CAPE of 1000-2000 J/kg but fairly strong deep-layer wind shear of 40-45 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some minor tree damage was reported in Damar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex of thunderstorms brings large hail and damaging wind gusts at rather odd time of day to portions of north central Kansas on May 9th. The severe thunderstorms that brought large hail up to half dollar size, and wind gusts around 60 MPH, actually developed over the high plains the previous evening and overnight. This activity arrived into NW portions of Phillips County shortly after 0900CDT, which is an unusual time for severe weather in this portion of the country. The MCS evolved into a mix of bowing segments and embedded supercells as they crossed north central Kansas and resulted in a mix of significant severe wind gusts and large hail. The strongest wind gusts ranged from 70 to 80 MPH, and the largest reported hail was up to tennis ball size. There is a climatological minimum in severe weather during 0600-1200 local time frame in this part of the country, so it is even more unusual that there were instances of both significant severe hail and wind. The severe portion of this MCS exited north central Kansas by around 1300CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported along this path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought conditions expanded across portions southeastern Kansas through the end of June. Little to no rainfall allowed drought conditions to deteriorate over most of southeastern Kansas during the month. Across southeastern Kansas Severe (D2) drought expanded or persisted with Moderate (D1) Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No significant rainfall occurred through the month which allowed for expansion of Severe (D2) Drought across the region through the month of June. The US Drought Monitor indicated the Severe (D2) Drought expanded southward across the northern three fourths of the county with the remainder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over southeast Kansas ahead of an approaching cold front during the evening of the 12th and moved across far southeastern Kansas through the morning of the 13th. Sporadic wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph downed trees and power poles from Arcadia and Fort Scott in Kansas. Flash flooding was reported in Pittsburg, Kansas where water rescues and stranded vehicles were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and powerlines were downed by high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was over the northern Rocky Mountains and Northern Plains and was bringing a cold front south into Kansas during the early evening hours of Independence Day. Temperatures heated up in advance of the front with dewpoints mostly in the 60s. A very moist and unstable atmosphere was present. CAPE values were generally near 4,000 J/kg ahead of the front, and 0 to 6 km wind shear values were around 35 knots. Mid-level lapse rates were generally 7 to 8 degrees C/km.||A storm developed and produced golf ball sized hail in Smith County in north central Kansas at 4:45 PM CDT. This was the biggest hail of the day in north central Kansas. This storm continued to grow in size and then split apart with one part of the storm moving to the west and the other part moving to the east. The east moving storm began moving southeast and formed into a bowing structure. This storm produced winds ranging from 58 mph to 71 mph in Phillips and Osborne Counties. The severe reports ended with a 68 mph wind gust in Phillips County just before 8 PM CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to ping pong to golf ball size was reported in Kensington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While some locations saw improvement from Extreme (D3) Drought by the end of July Extreme drought remained for portions of Bourbon and Crawford Counties. Severe (D2) Drought conditions expanded across portions southeastern Kansas through the end of July. What rainfall occurred during the month provided little improvement for drought conditions with some areas deteriorating over southeastern Kansas during the month. Across southeastern Kansas Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) drought expanded or persisted with Moderate (D1) Drought areas making up the remainder of the area. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No significant rainfall occurred through the month which allowed for expansion of Severe (D2) Drought across the region through the month of July. The US Drought Monitor indicated the Severe (D2) Drought expanded southward across the north eastern three fourths of the county with the remainder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During July 2023, the vast majority of this six-county North Central Kansas area spent the entire month assigned at least Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM), with all but a tiny fraction of the domain mired in at least Moderate Drought (D1) through the month as a long-term drought situation persisted. July marked the 18th-consecutive month with at-least-D2 plaguing portions of the local area, and the fourth-straight month with at least limited coverage of worst-off Extreme Drought (D3) and Exceptional Drought (D4). For North Central Kansas as a whole, July rainfall was somewhat variable, but fortunately few areas were overly-dry (see below for more specific precipitation details). Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics during, the majority of North Central Kansas remained stable/unchanged for the month. However, pockets of the four western-most counties (especially Rooks/Osborne) realized one-category improvement (including the removal of D4 from western Rooks), while a small portion of northern Jewell County saw one-category degradation. Digging into the details, the month began with the drought situation standing as follows: 1) Worst-off D4 lingered within western Rooks County...2) D3 encompassed most of primarily Osborne, Rooks and western Mitchell counties...3) D2 prevailed across most of the remainder of the area...4) Small zones of locally-best-off D1 resided in northwestern Phillips and southeastern Mitchell counties. During July, most categorical changes occurred during the first half of the month, including a modest reduction in D3 over primarily Rooks/Osborne counties and a small decrease of D1 in northwestern Phillips County. On a negative note, parts of northeastern Jewell County received a one-category degradation to D3. During the latter half of the month, the only change was the aforementioned one-category improvement (removal of D4) from western Rooks County. By month's end, the drought situation had evolved to the following: 1) Despite a slight reduction in its areal coverage, D3 was still present over much of Rooks, Osborne and western Mitchell counties, along with small parts of Phillips, Smith and Jewell...2) D2 still prevailed across the majority of the area...3) Limited zones of D1 remained over northwestern Phillips and far southeastern Mitchell counties...4) A small part of northwestern Phillips County boasted best-off D0 (marking the first time since late-May that any part of North Central Kansas was void of a D1-or-worse drought category).||Turning to July 2023 rainfall details, modest variability was the overall theme. The majority of North Central Kansas registered between 2.20-4.30 inches...generally 50-100 percent of normal. Unfortunately, at least three-fourths of the area registered at least slightly-below normal amounts, but thankfully no more than 10 percent of the domain checked in with less than half of normal (few places markedly dry). Per around 25 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, some of the lowest July tallies included: 1.14 in Formoso, 2.09 in Smith Center, 2.19 at Lovewell Dam and 2.65 near Burr Oak. On the more fortunate (but limited in scope) side of the measuring stick, a few of the highest, near-to-slightly-above-normal tallies featured: 6.16 at Kirwin Dam, 5.84 in Beloit, 5.32 at Plainville and 4.34 in Cawker City.
